topics.title: Dynamic response of first order liquid level system and RC circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL407 - TRANSDUCER AND MEASUREMENTS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Dynamic response of first order liquid level system and RC circuit ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **first-order system** is a dynamic system whose mathematical model is described by a **first-order ordinary differential equation** containing a single energy storage element. In the context of process instrumentation and control, the two canonical first-order systems studied in this experiment are: - **First-Order Liquid Level System (Non-Interacting Tank):** A single-tank system where the liquid height $h(t)$ in response to a step change in inlet flow rate $Q_i(t)$ exhibits exponential rise (or fall) to a new steady-state value, governed by a single time constant $\tau$. - **First-Order RC Circuit:** A series resistor-capacitor network in which the capacitor voltage $V_o(t)$ in response to a step change in input voltage $V_i(t)$ exhibits exponential charging (or discharging) behavior, characterized by a single time constant $\tau$. The **dynamic response** is the time-domain output behavior of a system when subjected to a standard test input (typically a step, impulse, or ramp), and it reveals critical performance parameters: **steady-state value**, **time constant ($\tau$)**, **process gain ($K$)**, and **transient behavior**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The core objective of this experiment is to demonstrate that **mathematically analogous systems behave identically in their dynamic response**, even though they belong to different physical domains (hydraulic vs. electrical). This is the foundation of **system analog simulation** used in modern control engineering. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy — "The Bathroom Bucket and the Shower Drain"** Imagine a **bucket of water** (representing the liquid tank) with a small **drain hole at the bottom** (representing the valve resistance). The bucket has a **cross-sectional area $A$** — this is the "capacity" to hold water. - If you pour water in at a constant rate $Q_i$ (open a tap fully), the level rises quickly at first, then **slows down** as the water pressure at the drain increases and more water flows out. Eventually, inflow equals outflow, and the level stabilizes. - A **bigger bucket** (larger $A$) takes longer to fill — it has a **larger time constant**. - A **wider drain** (smaller resistance $R$) means water escapes faster — it reaches steady state **quicker** (smaller $\tau$). Now compare this to **charging a capacitor through a resistor** (RC circuit): - A capacitor is like the bucket (stores energy/charge). - A resistor is like the drain (limits flow of charge/current). - The voltage across the capacitor rises the same way as the water level — fast at first, then slowing down, then settling at a final value. > [!NOTE] > **The Crucial Insight:** In both cases, the response is described by the **same mathematical equation**: > $$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot u(t)$$ > The only difference is what $y$, $u$, and $\tau$ physically represent! ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants | Physical Quantity | Liquid Level System | RC Circuit | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **Input (Driving Variable)** | Inlet flow rate $Q_i$ | Input voltage $V_i$ | m³/s / V | | **Output (Response Variable)** | Liquid height $h$ | Capacitor voltage $V_o$ | m / V | | **Storage Element (Capacitance)** | Tank cross-section $A$ | Capacitance $C$ | m² / F | | **Dissipative Element (Resistance)** | Valve resistance $R = \frac{dh}{dQ_o}$ | Resistance $R$ | s/m² / $\Omega$ | | **Time Constant ($\tau$)** | $A \cdot R$ | $R \cdot C$ | s | | **Process Gain ($K$)** | $R$ | $1$ (unity) | s/m² / dimensionless | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why First-Order? A system is classified as **first-order** if: 1. It contains **exactly one independent energy storage element** (one capacitor, one inductor, or one tank). 2. The relationship between input and output is governed by a **first-order linear ODE** with constant coefficients. 3. The **impulse response** is a single decaying exponential. 4. The **step response** is a single rising (or falling) exponential approaching a steady-state value. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of Response Characteristics For a general first-order system under step input $u(t) = U \cdot \mathbf{1}(t)$ (where $\mathbf{1}(t)$ is the unit step function): **Step 1 — Identify the governing ODE:** $$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot u(t)$$ **Step 2 — Apply the step input** $u(t) = U$ for $t \geq 0$: $$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot U$$ **Step 3 — Solve the homogeneous part** (transient): $$y_h(t) = C_1 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ **Step 4 — Find the particular (steady-state) solution:** $$y_{ss} = K \cdot U$$ **Step 5 — Apply initial condition** $y(0) = y_0$ (typically $0$ for a fully drained tank or uncharged capacitor): $$C_1 = y_0 - K \cdot U$$ **Step 6 — Combine to get the complete step response:** $$y(t) = K \cdot U \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + y_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ For a **zero initial condition** ($y_0 = 0$), this simplifies to: $$\boxed{y(t) = K \cdot U \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$ ### 2.3 Key Performance Parameters - **Time Constant ($\tau$):** The time required for the response to reach **63.2%** of its final steady-state value (since $1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$). Also, after $t = 5\tau$, the system is considered to have reached steady state (within 99.3%). - **Process Gain ($K$):** The ratio of steady-state change in output to the change in input. - **Settling Time ($t_s$):** The time to reach and stay within $\pm 2\%$ or $\pm 5\%$ of the final value. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all formulas below, the **absolute value / magnitude** is denoted by `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. **Bold $K$** refers to the process gain, not the unit Kelvin. | **S.No** | **Parameter / Equation** | **Liquid Level System** | **RC Circuit** | **Physical Meaning** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Governing ODE** | $A \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{h}{R} = Q_i$ | $RC \frac{dV_o}{dt} + V_o = V_i$ | System dynamics | | 2 | **Standard Form** | $\tau \frac{dh}{dt} + h = K_p Q_i$ | $\tau \frac{dV_o}{dt} + V_o = V_i$ | Canonical form | | 3 | **Time Constant $\tau$** | $\tau = A \cdot R$ | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Speed of response | | 4 | **Process Gain $K$** | $K = R$ | $K = 1$ | DC sensitivity | | 5 | **Transfer Function $G(s)$** | $G(s) = \dfrac{R}{A R s + 1} = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{RC s + 1} = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | Frequency-domain model | | 6 | **Step Response (Zero IC)** | $h(t) = R \cdot Q_i \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $V_o(t) = V_i \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Time-domain output | | 7 | **Steady-State Value** | $h_{ss} = R \cdot Q_i$ | $V_{o,ss} = V_i$ | Final output | | 8 | **Value at $t = \tau$** | $0.632 \cdot h_{ss}$ | $0.632 \cdot V_i$ | 63.2% point | | 9 | **Value at $t = 5\tau$** | $0.9933 \cdot h_{ss}$ | $0.9933 \cdot V_i$ | ~99% settled | | 10 | **Output at any time $t$** | $h(t) = h_{ss} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $V_o(t) = V_{ss} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Universal first-order form | | 11 | **Initial Slope** | $\left.\dfrac{dh}{dt}\right\vert_{t=0} = \dfrac{h_{ss}}{\tau}$ | $\left.\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}\right\vert_{t=0} = \dfrac{V_{ss}}{\tau}$ | Maximum rate of change | | 12 | **Rise Time $t_r$** (10% to 90%) | $t_r \approx 2.2 \tau$ | $t_r \approx 2.2 \tau$ | Quickness metric | | 13 | **Settling Time $t_s$** ($\pm 2\%$) | $t_s \approx 4 \tau$ | $t_s \approx 4 \tau$ | Speed of settling | | 14 | **Settling Time $t_s$** ($\pm 5\%$) | $t_s \approx 3 \tau$ | $t_s \approx 3 \tau$ | Speed of settling | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The analogy between hydraulic and electrical first-order systems is exploited extensively in: - **Process Control Industry:** Plant operators study RC circuit behavior on a small electronic simulator to predict the response of large chemical reactors, distillation columns, and storage tanks — saving time, cost, and risk. - **Analog Computing:** Before digital computers, electrical analog computers (using op-amps with RC networks) solved differential equations that modeled mechanical, thermal, and hydraulic systems. - **Biomedical Engineering:** Lungs (capacitance) and airways (resistance) form a first-order system; the same equations describe drug concentration decay in the bloodstream. - **Thermal Systems:** Heating a tank of water (thermal capacitance) via a heater (thermal resistance) is mathematically identical to charging a capacitor through a resistor. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Mathematical Model of the Liquid Level System **System Description:** Consider a single, open, vertical cylindrical tank with: - Constant cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) - Inlet volumetric flow rate $Q_i(t)$ (m³/s) — the input - Outlet volumetric flow rate $Q_o(t)$ (m³/s) — depends on liquid level - Liquid height $h(t)$ (m) — the output **Step 1: Volumetric Material Balance (Conservation of Mass)** The rate of accumulation of liquid in the tank equals the rate of inflow minus the rate of outflow: $$A \frac{dh(t)}{dt} = Q_i(t) - Q_o(t)$$ *Textual explanation:* The left side represents the rate at which volume is accumulating in the tank (cross-sectional area $A$ times the rate of rise of liquid surface $dh/dt$). The right side is the net inflow (inflow minus outflow), which by conservation of mass must equal the accumulation. **Step 2: Constitutive Relation for the Outlet Valve** For a **linear resistance valve**, the outflow rate is directly proportional to the hydrostatic head (liquid height) above the valve: $$Q_o(t) = \frac{h(t)}{R}$$ where $R$ is the **valve flow resistance** (s/m²), defined as the change in head required to cause a unit change in flow rate. *Textual explanation:* This is a linearized assumption valid for small deviations around an operating point. Higher liquid level produces greater pressure at the valve, causing more flow out — analogous to Ohm's law ($V = IR$) in electrical circuits. **Step 3: Substitute the Constitutive Relation into the Material Balance** $$A \frac{dh(t)}{dt} = Q_i(t) - \frac{h(t)}{R}$$ **Step 4: Rearrange into the Standard First-Order Form** Multiply both sides by $R$: $$A R \frac{dh(t)}{dt} = R \cdot Q_i(t) - h(t)$$ Bring the $h(t)$ term to the left: $$A R \frac{dh(t)}{dt} + h(t) = R \cdot Q_i(t)$$ **Step 5: Identify the Time Constant and Process Gain** Comparing with the canonical form $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K \cdot u$: $$\boxed{\tau_l = A \cdot R \quad \text{and} \quad K_l = R}$$ The final governing differential equation is: $$\boxed{A R \frac{dh(t)}{dt} + h(t) = R \cdot Q_i(t)}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation II — Mathematical Model of the Series RC Circuit **System Description:** A series circuit consisting of: - A resistor of resistance $R$ ($\Omega$) in series with - A capacitor of capacitance $C$ (F) - Input voltage $v_i(t)$ (V) applied across the series combination - Output voltage $v_o(t)$ (V) taken across the capacitor **Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** The sum of voltage drops around the closed loop equals the applied voltage: $$v_i(t) = v_R(t) + v_o(t)$$ *Textual explanation:* The input voltage $v_i$ drives current through the series combination. Part of the voltage is dropped across the resistor ($v_R$) and the remainder appears across the capacitor ($v_o$). **Step 2: Apply Ohm's Law to the Resistor** The voltage across the resistor is proportional to the current: $$v_R(t) = R \cdot i(t)$$ **Step 3: Apply the Capacitor Constitutive Relation** The current through a capacitor is proportional to the rate of change of voltage across it: $$i(t) = C \frac{dv_o(t)}{dt}$$ *Textual explanation:* Charge $q$ stored on a capacitor is $q = C v_o$. Since current is the rate of change of charge, $i = dq/dt = C \, dv_o/dt$. **Step 4: Substitute into KVL** $$v_i(t) = R \cdot \left[ C \frac{dv_o(t)}{dt} \right] + v_o(t)$$ **Step 5: Rearrange into Standard First-Order Form** $$R C \frac{dv_o(t)}{dt} + v_o(t) = v_i(t)$$ **Step 6: Identify the Time Constant and Process Gain** Comparing with the canonical form $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K \cdot u$: $$\boxed{\tau_e = R \cdot C \quad \text{and} \quad K_e = 1}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation III — Step Response of the Liquid Level System **Given:** A step input $Q_i(t) = Q_s \cdot \mathbf{1}(t)$ is applied at $t = 0$, with initial condition $h(0) = h_0$ (often $0$ for an empty tank). **Step 1: Write the ODE with the step input** $$A R \frac{dh(t)}{dt} + h(t) = R \cdot Q_s \quad \text{for} \; t \geq 0$$ **Step 2: Solve the Homogeneous Equation** The homogeneous part: $A R \frac{dh}{dt} + h = 0$, which gives: $$\frac{dh}{h} = -\frac{1}{A R} dt$$ Integrating: $$\ln \vert h \vert = -\frac{t}{A R} + C$$ $$h_h(t) = K_1 \cdot e^{-t/(AR)}$$ **Step 3: Find the Particular (Steady-State) Solution** At steady state, $dh/dt = 0$, so: $$h_{ss} = R \cdot Q_s$$ **Step 4: Apply the Initial Condition $h(0) = h_0$** The general solution is $h(t) = h_{ss} + K_1 e^{-t/\tau}$ where $\tau = AR$. At $t = 0$: $h_0 = h_{ss} + K_1$, so $K_1 = h_0 - h_{ss}$. **Step 5: Write the Complete Solution** $$h(t) = h_{ss} + (h_0 - h_{ss}) e^{-t/\tau}$$ For the special case of an **empty tank** ($h_0 = 0$): $$\boxed{h(t) = R \cdot Q_s \left(1 - e^{-t/(A R)}\right)}$$ **Step 6: Verify Limiting Behavior** - As $t \to 0$: $h(0) = R \cdot Q_s (1 - 1) = 0$ ✓ - As $t \to \infty$: $h(\infty) = R \cdot Q_s (1 - 0) = h_{ss}$ ✓ - At $t = \tau = AR$: $h(\tau) = 0.632 \cdot h_{ss}$ ✓ ### 3.4 Derivation IV — Step Response of the RC Circuit **Given:** A step input $v_i(t) = V_s \cdot \mathbf{1}(t)$ applied at $t = 0$, with $v_o(0) = 0$ (initially uncharged capacitor). **Step 1: Substitute into the governing ODE** $$RC \frac{dv_o(t)}{dt} + v_o(t) = V_s \quad \text{for} \; t \geq 0$$ **Step 2: Apply Laplace Transform** (alternative method) Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions: $$RC \cdot s \cdot V_o(s) + V_o(s) = \frac{V_s}{s}$$ $$V_o(s) (RC \cdot s + 1) = \frac{V_s}{s}$$ $$V_o(s) = \frac{V_s}{s (RC \cdot s + 1)}$$ **Step 3: Partial Fraction Decomposition** $$V_o(s) = \frac{V_s}{s} - \frac{V_s}{s + 1/(RC)}$$ *Textual explanation:* This decomposition is obtained by writing $\dfrac{1}{s(RC s + 1)} = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{RC s + 1}$ and solving for $A$ and $B$. Multiplying through: $1 = A(RC s + 1) + B s$. Setting $s = 0$: $A = 1$. Setting $s = -1/(RC)$: $B = -RC \cdot RC / RC = -1$ (after algebraic manipulation). The result is shown above. **Step 4: Apply Inverse Laplace Transform** Using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s}\right\} = 1$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s + a}\right\} = e^{-a t}$: $$v_o(t) = V_s - V_s \cdot e^{-t/(RC)}$$ **Step 5: Final Result** $$\boxed{v_o(t) = V_s \left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right)}$$ ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Liquid Level vs. RC Circuit | **Property** | **Liquid Level System** | **RC Circuit** | **Analogy** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **ODE Form** | $AR \dfrac{dh}{dt} + h = R Q_i$ | $RC \dfrac{dV_o}{dt} + V_o = V_i$ | Identical structure | | **Input Variable** | $Q_i(t)$ | $V_i(t)$ | "Effort" / "Flow" source | | **Output Variable** | $h(t)$ | $V_o(t)$ | State variable | | **Storage Element** | Area $A$ (m²) | Capacitance $C$ (F) | Energy storage | | **Dissipative Element** | Valve $R$ (s/m²) | Resistance $R$ ($\Omega$) | Energy dissipation | | **Time Constant $\tau$** | $A \cdot R$ | $R \cdot C$ | System inertia | | **Process Gain $K$** | $R$ | $1$ | DC amplification | | **Steady State** | $h_{ss} = R Q_s$ | $V_{o,ss} = V_s$ | Final value | | **Response Shape** | Exponential rise | Exponential rise | Mathematically identical | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Takeaway:** The transfer function structure $\dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ is **identical** for both systems. Therefore, any first-order system, regardless of physical domain, will exhibit the same normalized step response — this is the **principle of dynamic similarity** that allows analog simulation in control engineering. ### 3.6 Derivation V — Determination of System Parameters from Experimental Data **Method 1: From the step response curve** Given a recorded plot of $h(t)$ vs. $t$ (or $V_o(t)$ vs. $t$): **Step 1:** Identify the steady-state value $h_{ss}$ (or $V_{o,ss}$) — this is the asymptotic final value. **Step 2:** Find the **process gain**: $$K = \frac{h_{ss}}{Q_s} \quad \text{or} \quad K = \frac{V_{o,ss}}{V_s}$$ **Step 3:** Find the time at which the response reaches **63.2% of the steady-state value**: $$h(\tau) = 0.632 \cdot h_{ss} \quad \text{or} \quad V_o(\tau) = 0.632 \cdot V_{o,ss}$$ The corresponding time on the x-axis is the **time constant $\tau$**. **Method 2: From the tangent line at $t = 0$** The initial slope of the response is $\frac{h_{ss}}{\tau}$ (or $\frac{V_{o,ss}}{\tau}$). If you draw a tangent at $t = 0$ and extend it to intersect the horizontal line $y = h_{ss}$, the time at intersection is exactly $\tau$. **Method 3: From the transfer function pole** The time constant is the reciprocal of the magnitude of the pole: $$\tau = -\frac{1}{\text{Re}(p)} \quad \text{where} \; p \; \text{is the system pole}$$ For $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$, the pole is at $s = -\dfrac{1}{\tau}$, so $\tau = -\dfrac{1}{p}$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Liquid Level System — Physical Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR inletValve["Inlet Valve
Source Q_in"] tank[("Storage Tank
Area A, Height h")] outletValve["Outlet Valve
Resistance R"] sensor["Level Sensor
Transducer h_t"] recorder["Data Recorder
h vs t plot"] inletValve -->|"Q_in m3/s"| tank tank -->|"Q_out = h/R"| outletValve tank -.->|"Physical level h"| sensor sensor -->|"Electrical signal"| recorder ``` ### 4.2 RC Circuit — Electrical Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR src["DC Source
Step Input V_in"] resistor["Resistor R ohms"] capacitor[("Capacitor C Farads
Output V_out")] scope["Oscilloscope
or DMM"] src -->|"V_in"| resistor resistor -->|"current i"| capacitor capacitor -.->|"V_out across"| scope ``` ### 4.3 System Analogy Block Diagram — Hydraulic ↔ Electrical ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph hydraulic["HYDRAULIC DOMAIN"] Qi["Input Q_in
Flow Rate"] Rv["Valve R
Hydraulic Resistance"] At["Tank A
Hydraulic Capacitance"] hout["Output h
Liquid Height"] Qi --> Rv Rv --> At At --> hout end subgraph electrical["ELECTRICAL DOMAIN"] Vi["Input V_in
Voltage"] Re["Resistor R
Electrical Resistance"] Ce["Capacitor C
Electrical Capacitance"] Vout["Output V_out
Capacitor Voltage"] Vi --> Re Re --> Ce Ce --> Vout end Qi -. "ANALOGY" .- Vi Rv -. "R val = R elec" .- Re At -. "A = C" .- Ce hout -. "h = V_out" .- Vout ``` ### 4.4 Step Response Curve (Typical Shape) ```mermaid flowchart LR axisOrigin["0"] timeAxis["t axis"] valAxis["y axis"] ssLine["Steady State y_ss"] tauMark["t = tau
y = 0.632 y_ss"] curve["y t = y_ss 1 minus e to t over tau"] axisOrigin --- timeAxis axisOrigin --- valAxis ssLine --- curve tauMark --- curve ``` ### 4.5 Experimental Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB step1["Step 1: Set up tank and RC circuit"] step2["Step 2: Empty tank and discharge capacitor"] step3["Step 3: Apply step input
Open inlet valve to Qs
Switch DC source to Vs"] step4["Step 4: Record h t and V_out t
at regular intervals"] step5["Step 5: Plot response curves"] step6["Step 6: Identify steady state value"] step7["Step 7: Find 63.2% point
read time constant tau"] step8["Step 8: Calculate K and tau"] step9["Step 9: Compare liquid and
electrical responses"] step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9 ``` ### 4.6 Parameter Extraction Strategy — Visual Aid ```mermaid flowchart TB start["Recorded response curve y t"] readSS["Read final asymptotic value
y_ss = K times U"] read632["Locate y = 0.632 y_ss
on the curve"] readT["Read corresponding time
tau from t axis"] computeK["K = y_ss divided by U"] computeR["For liquid: R = tau divided by A"] computeC["For RC: C = tau divided by R"] done["Parameters obtained:
K, tau"] start --> readSS start --> read632 read632 --> readT readSS --> computeK readT --> computeR readT --> computeC computeK --> done computeR --> done computeC --> done ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, As per 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **Q1. Define the term "time constant" of a first-order system. What is its significance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **time constant $\tau$** of a first-order system is defined as the time required for the step response to reach **63.2% of its final steady-state value**, starting from zero initial condition. Mathematically, $\tau$ is the reciprocal of the magnitude of the system pole in the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$. **Significance:** 1. It characterizes the **speed of response** of the system — a smaller $\tau$ indicates a faster system. 2. It determines the **settling time** — the system is considered settled (within $\pm 2\%$) after approximately $4\tau$. 3. It determines the **initial rate of change** of the response — the initial slope is $\dfrac{y_{ss}}{\tau}$. 4. It is the **only system parameter** (along with gain $K$) needed to fully describe a first-order system. **(Marks distribution: Definition = 1, Mathematical statement = 1, Significance = 1)** --- **Q2. Establish the analogy between a first-order liquid level system and an RC circuit. List at least four analogous quantities.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Both systems are governed by mathematically identical first-order differential equations, with the following analogy: | Liquid Level System | RC Circuit | Shared Concept | |:---|:---|:---| | Inlet flow rate $Q_i$ | Input voltage $V_i$ | System input | | Liquid height $h$ | Capacitor voltage $V_o$ | System output | | Tank area $A$ | Capacitance $C$ | Energy storage element | | Valve resistance $R$ | Electrical resistance $R$ | Energy dissipating element | | Time constant $\tau = A R$ | Time constant $\tau = R C$ | System inertia | | Process gain $K = R$ | Process gain $K = 1$ | DC sensitivity | This mathematical equivalence allows the **simulation of hydraulic systems using electrical RC networks** in analog computing. **(Marks distribution: Statement of analogy = 1, Table of 4 pairs = 1, Practical implication = 1)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, As per 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order non-interacting liquid level system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tank of cross-sectional area $A = 0.5 \; \text{m}^2$ has a linear outflow valve with resistance $R = 4 \; \text{s/m}^2$. A step input flow rate of $Q_s = 0.2 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ is applied at $t = 0$. Determine: (i) The time constant (ii) The steady-state liquid height (iii) The liquid height at $t = 2 \; \text{s}$ and $t = 8 \; \text{s}$ (iv) The time required to reach 90% of the steady-state value (v) The initial rate of change of liquid height **(7 marks)** **Complete Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Transfer Function (7 marks):** Starting from the material balance equation: $$A \frac{dh(t)}{dt} = Q_i(t) - \frac{h(t)}{R}$$ Rearranging: $$A \frac{dh(t)}{dt} + \frac{h(t)}{R} = Q_i(t)$$ Multiplying through by $R$: $$AR \frac{dh(t)}{dt} + h(t) = R \cdot Q_i(t)$$ Taking the **Laplace transform** of both sides, assuming zero initial conditions ($h(0) = 0$): $$AR \cdot s \cdot H(s) + H(s) = R \cdot Q_i(s)$$ $$H(s) \left[AR \cdot s + 1\right] = R \cdot Q_i(s)$$ $$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{H(s)}{Q_i(s)} = \frac{R}{ARs + 1}}$$ Comparing with the standard form $\dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$: Time constant $\tau = AR$ and Process gain $K = R$. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $A = 0.5 \; \text{m}^2$, $R = 4 \; \text{s/m}^2$, $Q_s = 0.2 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. **(i) Time Constant:** $$\tau = A \cdot R = 0.5 \times 4 = \boxed{2 \; \text{s}}$$ **(ii) Steady-State Height:** $$h_{ss} = R \cdot Q_s = 4 \times 0.2 = \boxed{0.8 \; \text{m}}$$ **(iii) Height at $t = 2 \; \text{s}$ and $t = 8 \; \text{s}$:** Using $h(t) = h_{ss} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 0.8 \left(1 - e^{-t/2}\right)$: At $t = 2 \; \text{s}$: $h(2) = 0.8 \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = 0.8 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 0.8 \times 0.6321 = \boxed{0.5057 \; \text{m}}$ At $t = 8 \; \text{s}$: $h(8) = 0.8 \left(1 - e^{-4}\right) = 0.8 \times (1 - 0.0183) = 0.8 \times 0.9817 = \boxed{0.7854 \; \text{m}}$ **(iv) Time to reach 90% of steady state:** We need $h(t) = 0.9 \cdot h_{ss} = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72 \; \text{m}$. Setting up the equation: $$0.72 = 0.8 \left(1 - e^{-t/2}\right)$$ $$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t/2}$$ $$e^{-t/2} = 0.1$$ $$-\frac{t}{2} = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$ $$\boxed{t = 2 \times 2.3026 = 4.605 \; \text{s}}$$ **(v) Initial rate of change:** Differentiating $h(t) = 0.8 \left(1 - e^{-t/2}\right)$: $$\frac{dh(t)}{dt} = 0.8 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) e^{-t/2} = 0.4 \, e^{-t/2}$$ At $t = 0$: $$\left.\frac{dh}{dt}\right|_{t=0} = 0.4 \times e^{0} = \boxed{0.4 \; \text{m/s}}$$ (Alternatively: $h_{ss}/\tau = 0.8/2 = 0.4 \; \text{m/s}$.) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Derive the governing differential equation of a series RC circuit with input voltage $V_i$ and output voltage $V_o$ across the capacitor. Hence obtain its transfer function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A series RC circuit has $R = 10 \; \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 100 \; \mu\text{F}$. A step input voltage of $V_s = 5 \; \text{V}$ is applied at $t = 0$. Find: (i) The time constant (ii) The capacitor voltage at $t = 1 \; \text{s}$, $t = 2 \; \text{s}$, and $t = 5 \; \text{s}$ (iii) The initial charging current (iv) The time required for the capacitor to charge to $3 \; \text{V}$ **(7 marks)** **Complete Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Governing ODE and Transfer Function (7 marks):** Apply KVL around the loop: $$V_i(t) = V_R(t) + V_o(t)$$ With $V_R = iR$ and $i = C \dfrac{dV_o}{dt}$: $$V_i(t) = RC \frac{dV_o(t)}{dt} + V_o(t)$$ This is the required first-order governing ODE. Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions: $$V_i(s) = RC \cdot s \cdot V_o(s) + V_o(s) = V_o(s) (RC s + 1)$$ $$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{1}{RCs + 1}}$$ Time constant: $\tau = RC$. Process gain: $K = 1$. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $R = 10^4 \; \Omega$, $C = 100 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{F} = 10^{-4} \; \text{F}$, $V_s = 5 \; \text{V}$. **(i) Time Constant:** $$\tau = R \cdot C = 10^4 \times 10^{-4} = \boxed{1 \; \text{s}}$$ **(ii) Capacitor voltage at various times** (using $V_o(t) = 5(1 - e^{-t})$): At $t = 1 \; \text{s}$: $V_o(1) = 5(1 - e^{-1}) = 5 \times 0.6321 = \boxed{3.16 \; \text{V}}$ At $t = 2 \; \text{s}$: $V_o(2) = 5(1 - e^{-2}) = 5 \times 0.8647 = \boxed{4.32 \; \text{V}}$ At $t = 5 \; \text{s}$: $V_o(5) = 5(1 - e^{-5}) = 5 \times 0.9933 = \boxed{4.97 \; \text{V}}$ (practically fully charged) **(iii) Initial Charging Current:** At $t = 0^+$, the capacitor is uncharged ($V_o = 0$), so the entire source voltage drops across the resistor: $$i(0^+) = \frac{V_s - V_o(0)}{R} = \frac{5 - 0}{10^4} = \boxed{0.5 \; \text{mA}}$$ **(iv) Time to charge to 3 V:** We need $V_o(t) = 3 \; \text{V}$: $$3 = 5(1 - e^{-t})$$ $$0.6 = 1 - e^{-t}$$ $$e^{-t} = 0.4$$ $$t = -\ln(0.4) = 0.9163 \; \text{s}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 0.916 \; \text{s}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign error in ODE:** Students often write $\frac{dh}{dt} = Q_i - \frac{h}{R}$ without multiplying by $A$ to get accumulation. The correct form is $A \frac{dh}{dt} = Q_i - \frac{h}{R}$. (Lose 1 mark) > 2. **Confusing process gain with time constant:** $K = R$ is the gain (has units), and $\tau = AR$ is the time constant (in seconds). Do not swap them. (Lose 1 mark) > 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always specify units (m, s, V, A). A bare number is incomplete. (Lose 0.5 mark) > 4. **Mistake in time-constant estimation:** The 63.2% rule applies only when the initial value is zero. If $h(0) \neq 0$, the 63.2% point is measured from $h_0$ to $h_{ss}$, not from zero. (Lose 1 mark) > 5. **Improper Laplace transform usage:** When taking the Laplace transform, always state the **zero initial condition** assumption. Skipping this step costs marks in Part B derivations. (Lose 0.5 mark) > 6. **Sign convention for current in RC circuit:** Some students write $i = -C \frac{dV_o}{dt}$ which is wrong for the defined current direction. Follow the **passive sign convention**: current entering the positive terminal of a capacitor equals $C \, dV/dt$. (Lose 0.5 mark) ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam** - ✅ **First-order ODE canonical form:** $\tau \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = K u$ - ✅ **Liquid level time constant:** $\tau = A \cdot R$ (Area × Valve resistance) - ✅ **RC circuit time constant:** $\tau = R \cdot C$ (Resistance × Capacitance) - ✅ **Liquid level gain:** $K = R$ (valve resistance) - ✅ **RC circuit gain:** $K = 1$ (unity, since output is across the capacitor) - ✅ **Transfer function:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ (same form for both!) - ✅ **Step response formula:** $y(t) = K U \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ (zero initial condition) - ✅ **63.2% rule:** At $t = \tau$, response reaches $63.2\%$ of final value - ✅ **Settling time:** $4\tau$ for $\pm 2\%$ band, $3\tau$ for $\pm 5\%$ band - ✅ **Five-time-constant rule:** At $t = 5\tau$, system is $\sim 99.3\%$ settled (treat as steady state) - ✅ **Initial slope:** $\left.\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right|_{t=0} = \dfrac{y_{ss}}{\tau}$ - ✅ **Analogy pairs:** $Q_i \leftrightarrow V_i$, $h \leftrightarrow V_o$, $A \leftrightarrow C$, $R_v \leftrightarrow R_e$ - ✅ **Experimental method:** Apply step input → record $h(t)$ or $V_o(t)$ → plot curve → read $y_{ss}$ → find 63.2% point → read $\tau$ from time axis - ✅ **Graphical method (alternative):** Draw tangent at $t = 0$ → extend to intersect $y = y_{ss}$ → time at intersection is $\tau$ - ✅ **Energy storage principle:** A system is first-order iff it has **one** independent energy storage element - ✅ **Pole location:** System pole at $s = -1/\tau$ (always on the negative real axis for stable first-order systems) - ✅ **Linearity assumption:** Both models are valid only for **small deviations** around a steady operating point > 🎯 **Pro Tip for KTU 2024:** When asked to "show the analogy," always present it as a **side-by-side table** with the governing equation on top — examiners award full marks only when the structural similarity is explicitly demonstrated, not merely stated in words. Also remember to **draw the system diagram** wherever possible — visual representations fetch extra marks in the lab report and viva voce.

topics.title: Response Characteristics and V-I Characteristics of LDR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL407 - TRANSDUCER AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Response Characteristics and V-I Characteristics of LDR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)**, also known as a **photoresistor** or **photoconductive cell**, is a passive resistive transducer whose electrical resistance varies as an inverse function of the intensity of incident electromagnetic radiation (typically in the visible and near-infrared spectrum).

The LDR is fabricated from high-resistivity semiconductor materials such as **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)** or **Cadmium Selenide (CdSe)**, deposited as a thin serpentine film on a ceramic substrate. The phenomenon governing its operation is **photoconductivity** — the reduction in electrical resistance of a semiconductor when photons of sufficient energy strike its surface, liberating bound valence electrons into the conduction band.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):** *“An LDR is a passive photoconductive transducer that converts incident light energy into a corresponding change in electrical resistance, exhibiting a high 'dark resistance' (MΩ range) in absence of light and a low 'light resistance' (few hundred ohms) under bright illumination.”*

## 1.2 Physical & Electrical Specifications (KTU Lab Standard)

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Note |
|-----------|---------------|----------|
| **Dark Resistance ($R_D$)** | $\geq 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ | Resistance at 0 lux |
| **Light Resistance ($R_L$)** | $\leq 500 \text{ \Omega}$ | Resistance at 1000 lux |
| **Spectral Peak Response** | $\lambda_p \approx 540 \text{ nm}$ (CdS) | Green wavelength |
| **Response Time (Rise)** | $t_r \approx 10 \text{ ms}$ | Time to reach 63% final value |
| **Response Time (Decay)** | $t_d \approx 100 \text{ ms}$ | Slower due to trap states |
| **Power Dissipation** | $P_{max} = 100 \text{–} 200 \text{ mW}$ | Thermal limit |
| **Operating Temperature** | $-30^\circ\text{C}$ to $+75^\circ\text{C}$ | Lab ambient safe |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Water Tap" 🎛️

Imagine a **water tap connected to a tank**:
- The **flow of water (electric current)** depends on how much you **open the tap (resistance)**.
- Now, replace your hand with a **light-sensitive spring**:
  - In **darkness**, the spring is stiff and clamps the tap shut → **very high resistance (MΩ)**, almost no current flows.
  - In **bright light**, the spring relaxes, fully opening the tap → **very low resistance (a few hundred Ω)**, current flows freely.

> The LDR works exactly like this: **light is the "force" that opens the tap to let electrons through.**

## 1.4 Why We Study Both Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Response Characteristics** = $R$ vs. Illuminance $E$ (lux) → tells us *how sensitive* the device is.
> **V–I Characteristics** = $V$ vs. $I$ at fixed $E$ → tells us whether the device is **ohmic (linear)** or **non-ohmic (non-linear)** under operating conditions.

Both characteristics are essential because:
1. **Response** determines the suitability of the LDR for light-sensing applications (street lamps, camera meters, alarm circuits).
2. **V–I curve** confirms whether the LDR can be modeled as a simple resistor in the bias range or whether the **photoconductive linearity** breaks down at high voltage (Joule heating effect).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Photoconductivity Explained Step-by-Step

When a photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu$ strikes the CdS semiconductor, it can liberate a bound electron from the **valence band** into the **conduction band** — provided:

$$E_{ph} = h\nu \geq E_g$$

where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant $= 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- $\nu$ = frequency of incident light
- $E_g$ = band-gap energy of CdS $\approx 2.42 \text{ eV}$ (corresponding to $\lambda \approx 514 \text{ nm}$)

### Step-by-Step Photoconduction Mechanism

1. **Photon Absorption** → A photon with energy $\geq E_g$ is absorbed by a valence electron.
2. **Electron–Hole Pair (EHP) Generation** → The excited electron jumps to the conduction band, leaving a hole in the valence band.
3. **Carrier Density Increase** → Free electron concentration $n$ rises from intrinsic value $n_0$ to $n_0 + \Delta n$.
4. **Conductivity Enhancement** → Since $\sigma = q(n\mu_e + p\mu_h)$, the rise in $n$ causes $\sigma \uparrow$ and therefore $R \downarrow$.
5. **Recombination** → After $\tau$ (carrier lifetime, $\sim$ ms for CdS), the electron recombines, releasing energy as heat/photon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The number of free carriers generated per second is proportional to the **photon flux** $\Phi$ (photons/m²·s), which is directly proportional to the **illuminance $E$ (lux)**. Hence $R$ is a deterministic function of $E$: $R = f(E)$.

## 2.2 The Photoconductive Response Equation

The classical empirical relationship between LDR resistance and illuminance follows a **power law**:

$$R = A \cdot E^{-\alpha}$$

where:
- $R$ = LDR resistance in $\Omega$
- $E$ = illuminance in lux
- $A$ = constant (resistance at $E = 1$ lux), in $\Omega \cdot \text{lux}^{\alpha}$
- $\alpha$ = sensitivity exponent (typically $0.7$ to $0.9$ for CdS)

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln R = \ln A - \alpha \ln E$$

This is a **straight line** in the $\ln R$ vs. $\ln E$ domain — the foundation of the KTU lab graph.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly usable in KTU 2024 ESE & Continuous Evaluation** for PCICL407.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = A \cdot E^{-\alpha}$ | Power-law response of LDR | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $\log R = \log A - \alpha \log E$ | Logarithmic linearization (for graph) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\log R_1 - \log R_2}{\log E_2 - \log E_1}$ | Slope of log-log response | dimensionless |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta R}{\Delta E}$ | Static sensitivity | $\Omega / \text{lux}$ |
| 5 | $R_D$ | Dark resistance (at $E \approx 0$ lux) | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $R_L$ | Light resistance (at $E \approx 1000$ lux) | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $G = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Photoconductance | Siemens (S) |
| 8 | $V_D = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R}{R + R_s}$ | Voltage-divider output (sensor node) | V |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{V}{R(E)}$ | Current through LDR at illuminance $E$ | A |
| 10 | $V = I \cdot R(E)$ | Ohm's law with light-dependent $R$ | V |
| 11 | $V_D = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + R_s/R}$ | Alternate form of voltage divider | V |
| 12 | $t_{rc} = 1/(2\pi f_c)$ | Response time constant | s |

**Notation Note:** In formulas above, $\log$ denotes base-10 logarithm (KTU standard for Bode-style graphs) unless specified as $\ln$.

## 2.4 Voltage Divider — The Most Common Biasing Circuit

The LDR is rarely used alone; it is always embedded in a **voltage divider** or **Wheatstone bridge** so that the resistance change can be converted to a measurable voltage $V_D$:

$$V_D = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{\text{LDR}}}{R_{\text{LDR}} + R_s}$$

where $R_s$ is a fixed series resistor and $V_{CC}$ is the supply (typically $+5$ V in KTU lab).

- **At dark:** $R_{\text{LDR}} \to \infty$ → $V_D \to V_{CC}$
- **At bright light:** $R_{\text{LDR}} \to R_L$ (small) → $V_D \to 0$

This monotonic behavior makes the LDR ideal for **light-level threshold detection**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why LDR is Used |
|-------------|-----------------|
| **Automatic Street Lighting** | Low cost, no bias needed, MΩ range gives clean ON/OFF |
| **Camera Light Meters** | Spectral response near 540 nm matches human eye |
| **Solar Tracking Systems** | Differential pair of LDRs for direction sensing |
| **Intruder Alarms** | Sudden change in $R$ triggers the alarm |
| **Toy & Hobby Circuits** | Robust, cheap, easy to interface with op-amps |
| **Light-Activated Relays** | LDR + transistor switch = solid-state relay |

## 2.6 Limitations (Examiner's Favorites!)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often ask about LDR **disadvantages**. Memorize these:
> 1. **Slow response time** ($100$ ms decay) — unsuitable for high-frequency modulation.
> 2. **Non-linear response** — requires log amplification for linear lux output.
> 3. **Temperature dependence** — $R$ drifts with ambient temperature.
> 4. **Hysteresis** — value depends on whether light is increasing or decreasing.
> 5. **Spectral selectivity** — CdS peaks at green; poor in red/blue.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power-Law Response of an LDR

We begin from the fundamental photoconductivity equation. The number of electron-hole pairs generated per second per unit volume is:

$$G = \eta \cdot \Phi \cdot \alpha_{abs}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = quantum efficiency (dimensionless, $0 < \eta \leq 1$)
- $\Phi$ = photon flux density (photons / m² · s)
- $\alpha_{abs}$ = absorption coefficient (m⁻¹)

The excess carrier density in steady state is given by the **carrier continuity equation**:

$$\Delta n = G \cdot \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the carrier lifetime (s). The resulting change in conductivity is:

$$\Delta \sigma = q \cdot \Delta n \cdot \mu_e = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi \cdot \alpha_{abs} \cdot \tau \cdot \mu_e$$

The **illuminance** $E$ (lux) is related to photon flux by:

$$E = \Phi \cdot h\nu \cdot K$$

where $K$ is the photopic luminosity function (lm/W conversion). For a fixed spectrum, $E \propto \Phi$, so:

$$\Delta \sigma = q \cdot \mu_e \cdot \tau \cdot \eta \cdot \alpha_{abs} \cdot \frac{E}{h\nu K}$$

Defining the lumped constant $C = \dfrac{q \cdot \mu_e \cdot \tau \cdot \eta \cdot \alpha_{abs}}{h\nu K}$, we get:

$$\Delta \sigma = C \cdot E$$

The total conductivity of the LDR is $\sigma = \sigma_d + \Delta\sigma$, where $\sigma_d$ is the dark conductivity. The LDR resistance is:

$$R = \frac{L}{\sigma \cdot A} = \frac{L}{(\sigma_d + C \cdot E) \cdot A}$$

$$\boxed{R(E) = \frac{R_D}{1 + (C \cdot R_D / L \cdot A) \cdot E}}$$

For high illuminance ($\sigma_d \ll C \cdot E$), this simplifies to a **power law**:

$$R \approx A \cdot E^{-\alpha} \quad \text{with } \alpha \approx 1$$

This is the form used in KTU lab manuals for fitting experimental data.

## 3.2 Derivation: Slope $\alpha$ from Experimental Data (Log-Log Linearization)

Take the logarithm of $R = A \cdot E^{-\alpha}$:

$$\log R = \log A - \alpha \log E$$

This is a straight line $y = mx + c$ with:
- $y = \log R$
- $x = \log E$
- Slope $m = -\alpha$
- Intercept $c = \log A$

**Experimental procedure (KTU 2024 lab manual):**

1. Measure $R_i$ at illuminance $E_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ (typically 6–8 readings).
2. Tabulate $(E_i, R_i)$.
3. Compute $\log E_i$ and $\log R_i$.
4. Apply **least-squares linear regression** to find slope $-\alpha$ and intercept $\log A$.

Using two-point form for simplicity:

$$\alpha = \frac{\log R_1 - \log R_2}{\log E_2 - \log E_1}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose measurements are:

| $i$ | $E_i$ (lux) | $R_i$ (k$\Omega$) | $\log E_i$ | $\log R_i$ |
|-----|-------------|-------------------|------------|------------|
| 1   | 50          | 24.0              | 1.699      | 4.380      |
| 2   | 100         | 13.0              | 2.000      | 4.114      |
| 3   | 200         | 7.2               | 2.301      | 3.857      |
| 4   | 500         | 3.3               | 2.699      | 3.519      |
| 5   | 1000        | 1.8               | 3.000      | 3.255      |

**Step 1: Compute the slope using points 1 and 5:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\log(24000) - \log(1800)}{\log(1000) - \log(50)} = \frac{4.380 - 3.255}{3.000 - 1.699}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{1.125}{1.301} = 0.865$$

**Step 2: Compute the intercept:**

$$\log A = \log R + \alpha \log E = 4.380 + 0.865 \times 1.699 = 4.380 + 1.469 = 5.849$$

$$A = 10^{5.849} = 7.07 \times 10^5 \, \Omega \cdot \text{lux}^{0.865}$$

**Step 3: Verify the model at $E = 200$ lux:**

$$R_{pred} = 7.07 \times 10^5 \times (200)^{-0.865} = 7.07 \times 10^5 \times 0.00999 \approx 7064 \, \Omega = 7.06 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Measured: $7.2$ k$\Omega$ → **Error = 1.9%** ✅ Excellent fit.

## 3.3 Derivation: V–I Characteristics at Fixed Illuminance

At a fixed illuminance $E_0$, the LDR behaves as an **ordinary resistor** $R(E_0) = R_0$. Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the simple series circuit (LDR + ammeter, voltmeter across LDR):

$$V = I \cdot R_0$$

Therefore, the V–I curve is a **straight line through the origin** with slope $1/R_0$:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{1}{R(E_0)} \cdot I}$$

### Expected V–I Graph (KTU 2024 Standard Output)

The student plots $V$ on the Y-axis and $I$ on the X-axis for **three** illuminance levels: dark, room light, torch light.

| Curve | Illuminance | Slope ($1/R$) | Interpretation |
|-------|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Dark ($E_1 \to 0$) | Very small (steeply rising $V$ for tiny $I$) | $R$ very large ($\sim$ M$\Omega$) |
| 2 | Room light ($E_2 \approx 300$ lux) | Intermediate slope | $R \approx$ few k$\Omega$ |
| 3 | Torch light ($E_3 \approx 1000$ lux) | Steep slope | $R \approx$ few hundred $\Omega$ |

All three lines should be **linear and pass through origin**, confirming **Ohmic behavior** of the LDR at the operating voltage range.

### Non-Linearity Threshold

At very high voltage (typically $> 10$ V for typical CdS cells), **Joule heating** becomes significant:

$$P = V \cdot I = V^2 / R$$

The local temperature rise reduces the band gap, increasing carrier density, which **artificially lowers $R$** and causes the V–I curve to bend **upward** (steeper-than-linear $I$ for given $V$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Guideline:** Always keep $V_{CC} \leq 5$ V during the experiment to stay in the linear regime.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Lab Observation |
|----------|--------|---------|-----------------|
| Dark resistance | $R_D$ | $R(E \to 0)$ | Should be $> 1$ M$\Omega$ |
| Light resistance | $R_L$ | $R(E = 1000)$ | Should be $< 1$ k$\Omega$ |
| Sensitivity exponent | $\alpha$ | Slope of $\log R$ vs $\log E$ | $0.7 \leq \alpha \leq 0.95$ |
| Photoconductive gain | $G$ | $\tau / t_{tr}$ | $\gg 1$ (high gain at low E) |
| Static sensitivity | $S$ | $\Delta R / \Delta E$ | Maximum near mid-range |
| Linearity error | $\epsilon_L$ | $\frac{R_{meas} - R_{pred}}{R_{pred}} \times 100\%$ | Should be $< 5\%$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Functional Block Architecture

The KTU 2024 lab setup for the LDR experiment comprises a light source, the LDR in a voltage divider, and dual metering. The block-level signal flow is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source Variable Intensity] --> B[LDR CdS Cell in Dark Chamber]
    B --> C[Voltage Divider Network R and Rs]
    C --> D[Digital Multimeter for V_D]
    C --> E[Digital Multimeter for I]
    F[DC Power Supply 0 to 5 Volt] --> C
    G[Lux Meter Photometric Sensor] --> H[Data Logger and PC]
    B -.-> G
    H --> I[Graph Plot R vs E and V vs I]
```

**Interpretation of Flow:**
1. The **light source** is varied (torch, lamp with dimmer, or calibrated light box).
2. The **LDR** is mounted in a light-tight box with a port for illumination.
3. The **voltage divider** converts the resistance change to a measurable $V_D$.
4. The **lux meter** independently measures the actual illuminance $E$ (lux).
5. All readings are tabulated and plotted to extract the response and V–I characteristics.

## 4.2 Circuit Schematic (LDR in Voltage-Divider Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerRail["Vcc = +5 V DC"]
        V1[Positive Terminal]
    end
    subgraph Divider["Voltage Divider"]
        RL[LDR Resistance R_E]
        RS[Fixed Resistor Rs = 10 kilo Ohm]
    end
    subgraph Meters["Measurement Points"]
        VM[Voltmeter across LDR reads V_D]
        AM[Ammeter in series reads I]
    end
    subgraph GroundRef["Ground 0 V"]
        GND[Common Reference]
    end
    V1 --> RL
    RL --> VM
    RL --> RS
    RS --> AM
    AM --> GND
    VM -.Monitors.- RL
```

**Key Circuit Equations:**

$$V_D = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R(E)}{R(E) + R_s}$$

$$I = \frac{V_{CC}}{R(E) + R_s}$$

## 4.3 State Diagram — Reading Acquisition Procedure

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerOn
    PowerOn --> DarkCalibrate: Cover LDR Shield
    DarkCalibrate --> RecordRdark: Read V_D equals V_CC and I equals 0
    RecordRdark --> LightOn: Switch on lamp at minimum
    LightOn --> ReadLux: Note E from lux meter
    ReadLux --> RecordVandI: Tabulate V_D and I
    RecordVandI --> IncrementLux: Increase lamp intensity
    IncrementLux --> ReadLux: Lux less than 1000
    IncrementLux --> ComputeParams: Lux equals 1000 reached
    ComputeParams --> PlotGraphs: Plot R vs E log scale and V vs I linear scale
    PlotGraphs --> ExtractAlpha: Compute slope of log R vs log E
    ExtractAlpha --> [*]: Lab Report Complete
```

## 4.4 Reading-Preparation Checklist (Visual)

| Component | Purpose | KTU Spec |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| LDR (CdS) | Sensing element | Peak $\lambda = 540$ nm |
| Decade resistance box | Replaces $R_s$ (variable) | $100 \, \Omega$ to $100 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| DC source | Powers the divider | $0$–$5$ V regulated |
| Digital voltmeter (DVM) | Reads $V_D$ | $\pm 0.1$ mV resolution |
| Digital ammeter (DMA) | Reads $I$ | $\pm 1 \text{ μA}$ resolution |
| Lux meter | Reference illuminance | $0$–$2000$ lux range |
| Variable lamp | Light stimulus | Tungsten, dimmer-controlled |
| Optical bench | Fixed distance alignment | LDR–lamp at $20$ cm |
