# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**Define the direct piezoelectric effect. Name any two piezoelectric materials used in vibration transducers.**

**Model Answer:**
The **direct piezoelectric effect** is the phenomenon in which certain non-centrosymmetric crystals generate an electric charge across their faces when subjected to mechanical stress (force or pressure). The generated charge $Q$ is proportional to the applied force $F$ per the relation $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge constant.

Two piezoelectric materials commonly used:
1. **Quartz (SiO$_2$)** — natural crystal, highly stable, used in precision accelerometers.
2. **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** — synthetic ceramic, high $d$ constant, used in industrial accelerometers.

*Other acceptable materials: Barium Titanate (BaTiO$_3$), Tourmaline, Rochelle Salt, PVDF polymer.*

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]
**What is the principle of operation of a magnetic pickup tachometer?**

**Model Answer:**
A magnetic pickup tachometer operates on the principle of **varying magnetic reluctance** combined with **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**. A toothed disc rotates in front of a permanent magnet wound with a pickup coil. As each tooth approaches and recedes from the magnet, the **air-gap flux** in the magnetic circuit changes, inducing an EMF in the coil per $e = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$. Each tooth produces one voltage pulse; counting pulses per second yields the shaft RPM via $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester]

**A(i). [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a piezoelectric accelerometer. Derive the expression for its output voltage.

**Model Solution:**

A **piezoelectric accelerometer** is a vibration transducer that produces an electrical output proportional to the acceleration of the vibrating surface on which it is mounted.

**Block Diagram (Refer Section 4.1):**
Vibration Source → Piezoelectric Sensor → Charge Amplifier → Filter/Amplifier → Display (CRO/PC)

**Working Principle:**

1. A known **seismic mass** $m$ is attached to a piezoelectric crystal (PZT). The combination is housed in a metallic case with a mounting base.
2. When the base vibrates with acceleration $a$, the seismic mass exerts an **inertial force** on the crystal:

$$
F = m \cdot a
$$

3. By the **direct piezoelectric effect**, this force generates a charge:

$$
Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a
$$

4. The charge $Q$ is fed to a **charge amplifier** (op-amp with feedback capacitor $C_f$) which converts charge to voltage:

$$
V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f}
$$

5. Substituting the value of $Q$:

$$
V_{out} = -\frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_f}
$$

6. The signal is filtered (to remove high-frequency noise), amplified, and displayed on a CRO. The peak-to-peak voltage of the waveform gives the vibration amplitude.

**Sensitivity:**

$$
S = \frac{|V_{out}|}{a} = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_f} \quad [\text{V/(m/s}^2\text{)}]
$$

**Key Advantages:**
- Self-generating (no external power required)
- Wide frequency range (0.1 Hz to 20 kHz)
- Rugged and compact
- Linear output with acceleration

---

**A(ii). [7 Marks]** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a seismic mass of $8 \text{ g}$ and a charge sensitivity of $50 \text{ pC/g}$. It is connected to a charge amplifier with a feedback capacitor of $2 \text{ nF}$. Calculate (a) the voltage sensitivity in V/g and (b) the output voltage when the input acceleration is $5g$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Seismic mass $m = 8 \text{ g}$
- Charge sensitivity $S_Q = 50 \text{ pC/g} = 50 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/g}$
- Feedback capacitor $C_f = 2 \text{ nF} = 2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}$
- Input acceleration $a = 5g$

**(a) Voltage Sensitivity in V/g:**

The charge sensitivity relates to the piezoelectric charge constant and mass as:

$$
S_Q = d \cdot m
$$

For a charge amplifier, the output voltage is:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C_f}
$$

So voltage sensitivity is:

$$
S_V = \frac{S_Q}{C_f} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
S_V = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/g} = 25 \text{ mV/g}
$$

**(b) Output Voltage at $a = 5g$:**

$$
V_{out} = S_V \times a = 25 \text{ mV/g} \times 5g = 125 \text{ mV} = 0.125 \text{ V}
$$

**Final Answer:** $S_V = 25 \text{ mV/g}$ and $V_{out} = 125 \text{ mV}$ at $5g$ input.

---

### Question Choice B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, End Semester]

**B(i). [7 Marks]** Explain the principle and working of an IR range sensor with a neat block diagram. Discuss the triangulation method used in it.

**Model Solution:**

An **IR range sensor** is a non-contact distance measurement device that uses infrared light and the principle of **triangulation** to determine the distance of an object.

**Block Diagram (Refer Section 4.3):**
IR LED → Target → Receiver Lens → PSD → Amplifier/Filter → Demodulator → Output Voltage

**Working Principle:**

1. A **modulated IR LED** (typically at 850 nm and modulated at 40 kHz) emits a collimated infrared beam toward the target object.
2. The beam strikes the target and undergoes **diffuse reflection** in all directions.
3. A portion of the reflected light passes through a **receiver lens** and falls on a **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)**.
4. The spot's position on the PSD depends on the **angle of the reflected ray**, which in turn depends on the target's distance $d$:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{L}{d}
$$

where $L$ is the fixed baseline between emitter and detector.

5. The PSD produces a current proportional to the spot position; onboard electronics convert it to a **DC voltage**:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{K}{d} \quad \text{(inversely proportional to distance)}
$$

6. The demodulator rejects ambient light (which is not modulated at 40 kHz), improving the signal-to-noise ratio.

**Why Modulation?**
Modulating the IR beam at a specific frequency allows the receiver to **filter out sunlight and other ambient IR sources**, ensuring reliable distance measurement even in bright conditions.

**Advantages:**
- Non-contact and wear-free
- Fast response (~40 ms)
- Immune to surface colour (within reason)
- Compact and low-cost

---

**B(ii). [7 Marks]** A magnetic pickup tachometer with a 60-tooth wheel is used to measure the speed of a motor shaft. The frequency counter reads 1800 Hz. Calculate (a) the shaft speed in RPM and (b) the angular velocity in rad/s. If the wheel has a radius of 5 cm, also find (c) the linear velocity of a point on the rim.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of teeth $N = 60$
- Frequency counter reading $f = 1800 \text{ Hz}$
- Radius of wheel $r = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$

**(a) Shaft Speed in RPM:**

Using the formula:

$$
n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N} = \frac{60 \times 1800}{60} = 1800 \text{ RPM}
$$

**(b) Angular Velocity in rad/s:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot n}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 60\pi
$$

$$
\omega = 188.496 \text{ rad/s} \approx 188.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(c) Linear Velocity of Rim Point:**

$$
v = r \cdot \omega = 0.05 \times 188.496 = 9.425 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $n = 1800 \text{ RPM}$, $\omega \approx 188.5 \text{ rad/s}$, $v \approx 9.43 \text{ m/s}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Cumulative Loss: Up to 4 Marks Per Question):**
> 
> 1. **Unit Omission:** Writing "$S_V = 25$" without the unit "mV/g" — KTU examiners strictly deduct 1 mark for missing units.
> 2. **Sign Error in Charge Amplifier:** The output of an inverting amplifier is negative. KTU expects $|V_{out}|$ (magnitude) in calculations; writing the negative sign without the magnitude convention costs a mark.
> 3. **Confusing Sensitivity Types:** Charge sensitivity (pC/g) vs. Voltage sensitivity (mV/g) — students often interchange them. Memorise: $S_V = S_Q / C_f$.
> 4. **Wrong Tooth Count Formula:** Writing $f = 60n/N$ instead of $n = 60f/N$ — direction matters. The frequency $f$ is in Hz (pulses per second), so RPM = pulses per second × 60 / number of pulses per revolution.
> 5. **IR Sensor Linearity Assumption:** The relation $V_{out} = C_1/d$ is non-linear. If the question asks for "linear range," specify 20–60 cm for GP2D12; do NOT claim the full 10–80 cm range is linear.
> 6. **Skipping Block Diagram:** Part B questions worth 7 marks often require a "neat block diagram." A missing diagram typically costs **2 marks** even if the theory is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Piezoelectric Vibration Transducer
- [x] **Direct piezoelectric effect:** mechanical stress → electric charge
- [x] Governing equation: $Q = d \cdot F$
- [x] Charge amplifier output: $V_{out} = -Q / C_f$
- [x] Common materials: **PZT, Quartz, BaTiO$_3$, Tourmaline, PVDF**
- [x] Charge sensitivity units: **pC/g** or **pC/(m/s$^2$)**
- [x] Voltage sensitivity units: **mV/g** or **V/(m/s$^2$)**
- [x] Seismic mass $m$ converts acceleration $a$ to force $F = ma$
- [x] Charge amplifier is mandatory — piezo output is high-impedance charge source
- [x] Typical lab frequency range: **0.1 Hz to 20 kHz**
- [x] Calibration source: **calibration shaker with known $g$ input**

### Speed Measurement
- [x] Two main types: **magnetic pickup** and **optical (photoelectric)**
- [x] Magnetic pickup: based on **reluctance variation** and **Faraday's law**
- [x] Optical tachometer: based on **light interruption** by slotted disc
- [x] Universal formula: $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N}$ RPM
- [x] Standard disc: **60 teeth/slots** for direct frequency-to-RPM conversion
- [x] Output is a **pulse train**; Schmitt trigger shapes the pulses
- [x] Angular velocity: $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$ rad/s
- [x] Linear tip speed: $v = r \cdot \omega$ m/s
- [x] Range: 0 to 10,000 RPM (typical lab setup)
- [x] Frequency counter displays the value directly in Hz

### IR Range Sensor
- [x] Operating principle: **triangulation** of reflected modulated IR light
- [x] Wavelength: **850 nm** (typical IR LED)
- [x] Modulation frequency: **40 kHz** (rejects ambient light)
- [x] Detector: **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)**
- [x] Common model: **Sharp GP2D12** (10–80 cm range, 0.4–3.1 V output)
- [x] Output relation: $V_{out} = C_1 / d$ — **inverse, non-linear**
- [x] Best linearity in: **20–60 cm** sub-range
- [x] Update rate: ~25 Hz
- [x] Power supply: **+5 V DC regulated**
- [x] Triangulation geometry: $\tan(\theta) = L / d$
- [x] Calibration: plot $V_{out}$ vs. $d$ on **semi-log** or **log-log** paper (linearises the inverse relation)

### KTU Viva Quick-Fire Terms
- [x] "Piezoelectric effect" → Jacques and Pierre Curie, **1880**
- [x] "Charge amplifier" → inverting op-amp with feedback capacitor
- [x] "Self-generating transducer" → no external power needed
- [x] "Modulation in IR sensor" → immunity to ambient light
- [x] "Triangulation" → distance from angle of reflected ray
- [x] "Tachometer" → Greek: *tachos* (speed) + *metron* (measure)
- [x] "Reluctance" → magnetic resistance of the air gap
- [x] "Faraday's law" → $e = -N d\phi/dt$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of DC ammeter, voltmeter and wattmeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL407 - TRANSDUCER AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of DC ammeter, voltmeter and wattmeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Calibration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the process of comparing the readings of an instrument under test (IUT) with a **reference standard instrument** of known and traceable accuracy, under specified conditions, to evaluate, adjust, or document the **deviation (error)** between the indicated value and the true value of the measurand.

In KTU 2024 Scheme lab practice, the term **calibration** refers specifically to the experimental determination of the relationship between the **true value** (provided by standard instruments) and the **indicated value** (shown by the instrument under calibration) over the entire range of the instrument, followed by the construction of a **calibration curve** (also called the *error curve* or *correction curve*).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Grocer's Weighing Scale**
> Imagine a local grocer using a mechanical weighing scale to sell rice. Customers trust that **1 kg on the scale = exactly 1 kg of rice**. To verify this, the grocer places a **standard 1 kg weight** on the scale. If the pointer reads 0.98 kg, the scale has an error of −0.02 kg. The grocer then tests with 2 kg, 3 kg, and 5 kg standard weights, noting the deviation at each point. Plotting true vs. indicated values gives a **calibration curve** — this is exactly what we do in the lab with DC meters!

The **DC ammeter**, **DC voltmeter**, and **DC wattmeter** are the three fundamental DC measuring instruments. Like the weighing scale, they must be periodically verified against standard references because their internal permanent magnets age, springs weaken, and coil resistances change — leading to **drift** in accuracy.

### 1.3 Definitions of the Three DC Instruments

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC Ammeter** — An instrument designed to measure **direct current (DC) in Amperes (A)**. It is essentially a PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil) galvanometer with a low-value **shunt resistance** connected in parallel to extend its current range.
>
> **DC Voltmeter** — An instrument designed to measure **DC potential difference in Volts (V)**. It is a PMMC galvanometer with a high-value **multiplier (series) resistance** connected in series to extend its voltage range.
>
> **DC Wattmeter** — An instrument designed to measure **DC electrical power in Watts (W)**. It may be a **dynamometer-type (electrodynamic)** wattmeter or a **PMMC voltmeter–ammeter combination**, computing power as $P = V \times I$.

### 1.4 Why Calibration is Necessary (Syllabus Highlight)

- **Manufacturing tolerances** cause each instrument to have small deviations.
- **Aging of components** (magnet flux, spring constant, shunt drift) alters the response.
- **To establish traceability** to national/international standards (NABL, ISO/IEC 17025).
- **To assign an accuracy class** to the instrument.
- **To obtain the correction factor** at any desired reading.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Instruments Used

| Instrument Under Test | Standard Reference Instrument |
| :--- | :--- |
| DC Ammeter | Standard DC ammeter (potentiometer-based) or calibrated DMM |
| DC Voltmeter | Standard DC voltmeter or DC potentiometer |
| DC Wattmeter | Standard wattmeter or $V \times I$ computed from standard V and I meters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The accuracy of the **standard instrument** must be at least **4 to 10 times better** than the instrument under calibration (as per ISO/IEC 17025 norms) for a valid calibration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of PMMC Movement (Common to Ammeter & Voltmeter)

The DC ammeter and DC voltmeter in this experiment are almost always based on the **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC)** mechanism. Its operating principle is the **D'Arsonval galvanometer action**:

When a current-carrying coil is placed in a uniform radial magnetic field, it experiences a torque proportional to the current.

The deflecting torque is:

$$\tau_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$

The controlling torque (from the spiral springs) is:

$$\tau_c = K \cdot \theta$$

At equilibrium, $\tau_d = \tau_c$, giving the **deflection equation**:

$$\theta = \frac{N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A}{K}$$

This shows that the **deflection is directly proportional to current** — making PMMC inherently **linear** and suitable for DC measurement.

### 2.2 Ammeter Calibration — Theoretical Foundation

A DC ammeter measures the current flowing through a circuit. The current $I$ entering the meter splits into two parallel paths:

- Current $I_m$ through the PMMC movement (with internal resistance $R_m$)
- Current $I_s$ through the **shunt resistance** $R_s$

By current division:

$$I_s = I \cdot \frac{R_m}{R_m + R_s}$$

$$I_m = I \cdot \frac{R_s}{R_m + R_s}$$

The **shunt multiplying factor** $m$ is defined as:

$$m = \frac{I}{I_m} = 1 + \frac{R_m}{R_s}$$

Solving for the required shunt resistance:

$$R_s = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}$$

### 2.3 Voltmeter Calibration — Theoretical Foundation

A DC voltmeter is a PMMC movement in series with a high-value **multiplier resistor** $R_s$. The total circuit resistance is $R_m + R_s$, and the full-scale deflection current $I_{fsd}$ produces the rated voltage:

$$V = I_{fsd} \cdot (R_m + R_s)$$

The **multiplication factor** $n$ for a voltmeter is:

$$n = \frac{V}{V_m} = 1 + \frac{R_s}{R_m}$$

Therefore, the required multiplier resistance is:

$$R_s = R_m \cdot (n - 1)$$

### 2.4 Wattmeter Calibration — Theoretical Foundation

For DC circuits, real power is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

A **dynamometer (electrodynamic) wattmeter** has two coils:

- **Current coil (CC)** — connected in series with the load, carries load current $I$.
- **Potential coil (PC)** — connected in parallel with the load, carries a current proportional to voltage $V$.

The deflecting torque is proportional to the product of the currents in the two coils:

$$\theta \propto V \cdot I = P$$

For DC circuits, calibration is done by **comparison with a standard wattmeter** OR by computing $P_{std} = V_{std} \times I_{std}$ using standard V and I meters.

### 2.5 Error Analysis — The Core of Calibration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **entire lab experiment** revolves around the following five error-related quantities. Memorize them perfectly.

**True Value** ($A_t$) — The value indicated by the standard reference instrument.
**Indicated Value** ($A_i$) — The value indicated by the instrument under test.
**Absolute Error** ($e$) — The numerical deviation:

$$e = A_i - A_t$$

**Correction** ($c$) — The value that must be added algebraically to the indicated value to obtain the true value:

$$c = A_t - A_i = -e$$

**Percentage Error** (% Error):

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{A_i - A_t}{A_t} \times 100$$

**Correction Factor (CF)** — The ratio of true value to indicated value:

$$CF = \frac{A_t}{A_i}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Absolute Error | $e = A_i - A_t$ | Same as measurand (A, V, W) | Sign indicates direction |
| Correction | $c = A_t - A_i$ | Same as measurand | Always opposite sign of error |
| Percentage Error | $\%e = \dfrac{A_i - A_t}{A_t} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Used to classify accuracy |
| Correction Factor | $CF = \dfrac{A_t}{A_i}$ | Dimensionless | Used in computations |
| Shunt Resistance (Ammeter) | $R_s = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $m = I/I_m$ |
| Multiplier Resistance (Voltmeter) | $R_s = R_m(n - 1)$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $n = V/V_m$ |
| DC Power | $P = V \times I$ | Watts (W) | $V$ in Volts, $I$ in Amps |
| True Power (Standard) | $P_t = V_t \times I_t$ | Watts (W) | Both $V_t$ and $I_t$ are standard values |
| Deflection Equation (PMMC) | $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$ | Radians | Linear scale, $\theta \propto I$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Calibration of DC meters is essential in **battery testing stations**, **DC motor test benches**, **solar PV power plants**, **electroplating industries**, and **railway traction systems** — all of which operate on DC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expression for Shunt Resistance in a DC Ammeter

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the shunt resistance $R_s$ required to extend the range of a PMMC ammeter from $I_m$ (full-scale deflection current of the movement) to a new full-scale value $I$.

**Given:**

- Internal resistance of PMMC movement: $R_m$
- Full-scale deflection current of movement: $I_m$
- Desired full-scale range of ammeter: $I$ (where $I > I_m$)
- Shunt resistance: $R_s$ connected in **parallel** with the movement.

**Circuit:** The shunt $R_s$ is connected in parallel with the PMMC coil of resistance $R_m$. Total current $I$ enters the combination and divides into $I_m$ (through movement) and $I_s$ (through shunt).

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):**
The total current entering the parallel combination equals the sum of the branch currents:

$$I = I_m + I_s$$

Solving for the shunt current:

$$I_s = I - I_m$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**
The voltage across the movement equals the voltage across the shunt (they are in parallel):

$$V_m = V_s$$

$$I_m \cdot R_m = I_s \cdot R_s$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $I_s$ from Step 1 into the parallel voltage equation:**

$$I_m \cdot R_m = (I - I_m) \cdot R_s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_s$:**

$$R_s = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the multiplying factor $m$:**

The multiplying factor is defined as the ratio of the new full-scale current to the original movement current:

$$m = \frac{I}{I_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = m \cdot I_m$$

Substitute into the expression for $R_s$:

$$R_s = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{m \cdot I_m - I_m} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I_m (m - 1)}$$

Canceling $I_m$ from numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{R_s = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

- As $m$ increases (larger current range required), $R_s$ **decreases**.
- For a 10× range extension ($m = 10$), $R_s = R_m/9$.
- For a 100× range extension ($m = 100$), $R_s = R_m/99$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Expression for Multiplier Resistance in a DC Voltmeter

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the multiplier (series) resistance $R_s$ required to extend the range of a PMMC voltmeter from $V_m$ to $V$.

**Given:**

- Internal resistance of PMMC movement: $R_m$
- Full-scale voltage of bare movement: $V_m = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m$
- Desired full-scale voltage: $V$ (where $V > V_m$)
- Multiplier resistance: $R_s$ connected in **series** with the movement.

**Step 1 — Define the full-scale deflection current:**

The same current $I_{fsd}$ flows through the series combination at full-scale:

$$I_{fsd} = \frac{V_m}{R_m} = \frac{V}{R_m + R_s}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply:**

$$V \cdot R_m = V_m \cdot (R_m + R_s)$$

**Step 3 — Expand and isolate $R_s$:**

$$V \cdot R_m = V_m \cdot R_m + V_m \cdot R_s$$

$$V \cdot R_m - V_m \cdot R_m = V_m \cdot R_s$$

$$R_m (V - V_m) = V_m \cdot R_s$$

**Step 4 — Define the multiplying factor $n$:**

$$n = \frac{V}{V_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = n \cdot V_m$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$R_s = \frac{R_m (n \cdot V_m - V_m)}{V_m} = \frac{R_m \cdot V_m (n - 1)}{V_m}$$

$$\boxed{R_s = R_m (n - 1)}$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity check:**

The **voltmeter sensitivity** (ohms per volt) is defined as:

$$S_v = \frac{1}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{R_m + R_s}{V} = \frac{1}{I_{fsd}}$$

For a 50 µA movement, $S_v = 20{,}000 \; \Omega/\text{V}$. A higher sensitivity voltmeter draws less current and produces less loading error.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: True Power and Percentage Error in DC Wattmeter Calibration

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the true power in a DC circuit and the percentage error in a wattmeter being calibrated.

**Given:** A DC load is connected to a regulated DC supply. A voltmeter (standard) and an ammeter (standard) are connected to measure the load's true voltage and true current. A wattmeter under test is also connected in the circuit.

**Step 1 — True Power Calculation:**

The **true power** is computed from the standard instruments' readings:

$$P_t = V_t \times I_t$$

where $V_t$ is the **standard voltmeter** reading (in Volts) and $I_t$ is the **standard ammeter** reading (in Amperes).

**Step 2 — Indicated Power from Wattmeter Under Test:**

The wattmeter under calibration directly displays its reading:

$$P_i = \text{(Wattmeter reading in Watts)}$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Error in Power:**

$$e_P = P_i - P_t$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Error in Power:**

$$\% \, e_P = \frac{P_i - P_t}{P_t} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Correction Factor for Power:**

$$CF_P = \frac{P_t}{P_i}$$

**Step 6 — Typical Numerical Example (Worked):**

Suppose at a calibration point:
- Standard ammeter reads: $I_t = 2.50 \; \text{A}$
- Standard voltmeter reads: $V_t = 24.0 \; \text{V}$
- Wattmeter under test reads: $P_i = 58.5 \; \text{W}$

Then:

$$P_t = 2.50 \times 24.0 = 60.0 \; \text{W}$$

$$e_P = 58.5 - 60.0 = -1.5 \; \text{W}$$

$$\% \, e_P = \frac{58.5 - 60.0}{60.0} \times 100 = -2.5 \; \%$$

$$CF_P = \frac{60.0}{58.5} = 1.0256$$

This means the wattmeter **under-reads by 2.5 %** at this point.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Calibration Procedure for All Three Meters

| Step | DC Ammeter | DC Voltmeter | DC Wattmeter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Power ON the regulated DC supply and standard instruments | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 2. Connect the meter under test **in series** with the load | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ (CC in series) |
| 3. Connect the meter under test **in parallel** with the load | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ (PC in parallel) |
| 4. Vary the load (using a rheostat) in 5–6 equal steps | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 5. Record standard $A_t$ and indicated $A_i$ at each step | ✓ (current) | ✓ (voltage) | ✓ ($V_t, I_t, P_i$) |
| 6. Calculate $e$, $\%e$, and $CF$ at each step | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 7. Plot **calibration curve**: $A_t$ (x-axis) vs. $A_i$ (y-axis) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 8. Plot **error curve**: True value (x-axis) vs. $\%e$ (y-axis) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 9. Determine the **accuracy class** of the instrument | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — General Calibration Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["STIMULUS SOURCE"]
        DCPS["Regulated DC Power Supply 0-30V / 0-5A"]
    end
    subgraph REF["REFERENCE SIDE"]
        SAM["Standard DC Ammeter"]
        SVM["Standard DC Voltmeter"]
    end
    subgraph TEST["INSTRUMENT UNDER TEST"]
        TAM["DC Ammeter Under Calibration"]
        TVM["DC Voltmeter Under Calibration"]
        TWM["DC Wattmeter Under Calibration"]
    end
    subgraph LOAD["CONTROLLED LOAD"]
        RHEO["Rheostat / Resistive Load Bank"]
    end
    DCPS --> RHEO
    RHEO --> SAM
    SAM --> TAM
    TAM --> RHEO
    DCPS --> SVM
    SVM --> TVM
    TVM --> RHEO
    DCPS --> TWM
    TWM --> RHEO
```

---

### 4.2 Detailed Circuit — DC Ammeter Calibration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    POS["DC Supply Positive Terminal"]
    NEG["DC Supply Negative Terminal"]
    SW["SPST Switch"]
    F1["Ammeter Under Test IUT"]
    F2["Standard Ammeter REF"]
    F3["Rheostat Load Variable R"]
    POS --> SW
    SW --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> NEG
    style POS fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style NEG fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style F1 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style F2 fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style F3 fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
```

**Working Logic (Step-by-step interpretation):**
- The DC supply provides a stable input voltage.
- The ammeter under test (IUT) and standard ammeter are connected in **series**, so the same current flows through both.
- The rheostat varies the load resistance, hence varying the current in **5–6 equal steps** from 0 to full-scale.
- At each step, the **standard ammeter** gives the true value $I_t$, and the **IUT** gives the indicated value $I_i$.

---

### 4.3 Detailed Circuit — DC Voltmeter Calibration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    POS["DC Supply Positive"]
    NEG["DC Supply Negative"]
    SW["SPST Switch"]
    RHEO["Rheostat / Load Resistor"]
    SP1["Standard Voltmeter REF connected in parallel"]
    SP2["Voltmeter Under Test IUT connected in parallel"]
    POS --> SW
    SW --> RHEO
    RHEO --> NEG
    POS --> SP1
    SP1 --> NEG
    POS --> SP2
    SP2 --> NEG
    style POS fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style NEG fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style SP1 fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style SP2 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style RHEO fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
```

**Working Logic:**
- The voltmeter under test and the standard voltmeter are connected in **parallel** across the load, so both measure the same voltage.
- The rheostat (or simply varying the supply voltage) changes the voltage across the load in steps.
- At each step, $V_t$ (standard) and $V_i$ (IUT) are recorded.

---

### 4.4 Detailed Circuit — DC Wattmeter Calibration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    POS["DC Supply Positive"]
    NEG["DC Supply Negative"]
    SW["SPST Switch"]
    CC["Wattmeter Current Coil CC"]
    LOAD["Rheostat Load"]
    SAM["Standard Ammeter in series with CC"]
    SVM["Standard Voltmeter in parallel with Load"]
    PC["Wattmeter Potential Coil PC connected across Load"]
    POS --> SW
    SW --> SAM
    SAM --> CC
    CC --> LOAD
    LOAD --> NEG
    SVM --> POS
    SVM --> NEG
    PC --> POS
    PC --> NEG
    style POS fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style NEG fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style CC fill:#fbf,stroke:#333
    style PC fill:#bff,stroke:#333
    style SAM fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style SVM fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style LOAD fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
```

**Working Logic:**
- The **current coil (CC)** of the wattmeter carries the load current — connected in series with the load.
- The **potential coil (PC)** is connected in parallel across the load — measures voltage.
- The **standard ammeter** (in series) gives $I_t$.
- The **standard voltmeter** (in parallel) gives $V_t$.
- The wattmeter under test directly displays $P_i$.
- True power $P_t = V_t \times I_t$.

---

### 4.5 Calibration Data Flow — Error Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Step 1 Vary Load / Supply"] --> B1["Step 2 Record Ai and At"]
    B1 --> C1["Step 3 Compute Error e = Ai - At"]
    C1 --> D1["Step 4 Compute percent Error"]
    D1 --> E1["Step 5 Compute Correction Factor CF = At / Ai"]
    E1 --> F1["Step 6 Tabulate Results"]
    F1 --> G1["Step 7 Plot Calibration Curve At vs Ai"]
    G1 --> H1["Step 8 Plot Error Curve True Value vs percent Error"]
    H1 --> I1["Step 9 Determine Accuracy Class"]
    style A1 fill:#ffe
    style I1 fill:#cfc
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **calibration curve** is ideally a **straight line at 45°** if there is no error. Any deviation from this line quantifies the error. The **error curve** ideally coincides with the **x-axis** (zero error) — any vertical deviation is the percentage error at that point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam Style — Short Answer Questions]**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question]** Define calibration. Why is it necessary to calibrate a DC measuring instrument periodically?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Calibration is the process of comparing the readings of an instrument under test (IUT) with a standard reference instrument of known higher accuracy, under specified conditions, to determine the deviation (error) between the indicated value and the true value of the measurand. *(1.5 marks)*

Calibration is necessary because:
- Instrument components age with time (magnet flux decreases, spring constant changes, shunt resistance drifts), causing accuracy degradation. *(0.5 mark)*
- It establishes traceability to national/international standards. *(0.5 mark)*
- It verifies whether the instrument meets its specified accuracy class for continued use. *(0.5 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question]** Define the following terms with reference to instrument calibration: (i) Absolute Error, (ii) Percentage Error, (iii) Correction Factor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Absolute Error (e):** The algebraic difference between the indicated value and the true value of the measurand.

$$e = A_i - A_t \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

(ii) **Percentage Error (%e):** The ratio of the absolute error to the true value, expressed as a percentage.

$$\%e = \frac{A_i - A_t}{A_t} \times 100 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

(iii) **Correction Factor (CF):** The ratio of the true value to the indicated value of the measurand.

$$CF = \frac{A_t}{A_i} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure for calibrating a **DC ammeter** using a standard ammeter. How is the calibration curve plotted? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
- DC regulated power supply (0–30 V, 0–5 A)
- Ammeter under test (IUT)
- Standard ammeter (with accuracy at least 4× better than IUT)
- Rheostat (suitable rating)
- Connecting wires and SPST switch

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to Section 4.2 — series connection of IUT and standard ammeter, in series with the load and supply].

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Make the connections as shown in the circuit diagram. Ensure proper polarity.
2. Keep the rheostat at its **maximum resistance** position before switching on.
3. Switch ON the DC supply. The voltmeter indication confirms the supply.
4. Slowly **decrease the rheostat resistance** to increase the current in steps.
5. At each step (5–6 points from 0 to full-scale), record the **standard ammeter reading $I_t$** and the **ammeter under test reading $I_i$**.
6. Switch OFF the supply after the last reading.

**Calibration Curve (2 marks):**
- **X-axis:** True value $I_t$ (from standard ammeter).
- **Y-axis:** Indicated value $I_i$ (from IUT).
- Draw a **smooth curve** through the plotted points. The ideal curve is a **45° straight line**.
- The **deviation** of the curve from the ideal line quantifies the error at each point.

The percentage error $\%e = \dfrac{I_i - I_t}{I_t} \times 100$ is also plotted as a separate **error curve** to identify the maximum deviation.

---

**Q.A (b)** Derive the expression for the **shunt resistance** required to extend the range of a PMMC ammeter. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** PMMC movement with internal resistance $R_m$, full-scale deflection current $I_m$, required new full-scale current $I$, shunt resistance $R_s$. *(1 mark)*

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are:

Step 1 (KCL): $I = I_m + I_s \Rightarrow I_s = I - I_m$

Step 2 (KVL across parallel combination): $I_m R_m = I_s R_s$

Step 3 (Substitution): $I_m R_m = (I - I_m) R_s$

Step 4 (Multiplying factor): $m = I/I_m \Rightarrow I = m I_m$

Step 5 (Final boxed result):

$$R_s = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The shunt resistance $R_s$ is inversely proportional to $(m-1)$. As the range extension factor $m$ increases, the shunt value decreases. For example, extending from 1 mA to 1 A requires $m = 1000$, giving $R_s = R_m/999$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style — Alternative Choice]**

**Q.B (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the calibration of a **DC voltmeter**. Also plot the calibration curve and comment on the accuracy class. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus (1 mark):** DC regulated supply, voltmeter under test (IUT), standard voltmeter, rheostat, SPST switch, connecting wires.

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to Section 4.3 — standard voltmeter and IUT both connected in parallel across the load].

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Connect the standard voltmeter and the IUT in **parallel** across the rheostat load.
2. Switch ON the supply and slowly **increase the supply voltage** in 5–6 steps from 0 V to full-scale.
3. At each step, record $V_t$ (standard) and $V_i$ (IUT).
4. Tabulate $V_t$, $V_i$, $e = V_i - V_t$, $\%e$, and $CF = V_t / V_i$.
5. Switch OFF the supply after the final reading.

**Calibration Curve and Accuracy Class (2 marks):**
- Plot $V_t$ vs. $V_i$ — ideally a 45° straight line.
- Calculate the **maximum percentage error** observed.
- The **accuracy class** is the maximum permissible error, e.g., a class 1.0 voltmeter has $\pm 1\%$ maximum error.

Example: If the maximum error is found to be +0.8 %, the voltmeter meets **class 1.0** specifications.

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the calibration procedure of a **DC wattmeter** using standard ammeter and standard voltmeter. Show the method of calculating true power and percentage error. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (1 mark):** For a DC load, true power $P_t = V_t \times I_t$. The wattmeter under test displays $P_i$ directly. The percentage error is computed and the calibration curve is plotted.

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to Section 4.4 — current coil in series, potential coil in parallel, with standard ammeter in series and standard voltmeter in parallel with the load].

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Make the connections as per the circuit diagram.
2. Vary the rheostat in 5–6 steps to vary the load current.
3. At each step, record:
   - $I_t$ from the standard ammeter (in Amperes).
   - $V_t$ from the standard voltmeter (in Volts).
   - $P_i$ from the wattmeter under test (in Watts).
4. Compute $P_t = V_t \times I_t$ at each step.
5. Calculate $e_P = P_i - P_t$, $\%e_P = \dfrac{P_i - P_t}{P_t} \times 100$, and $CF_P = P_t / P_i$.

**Error Plot and Result (2 marks):**
- Plot the calibration curve with $P_t$ on the x-axis and $P_i$ on the y-axis.
- The maximum percentage error determines the accuracy class of the wattmeter.
- For example, a wattmeter with maximum error of +0.6 % qualifies for **class 0.5** or **class 1.0** depending on the specification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students often confuse the sign of "error" vs. "correction". Remember: **Error = $A_i - A_t$**, but **Correction = $A_t - A_i$** (always opposite sign). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks per question.
>
> 2. **Confusing Ammeter and Voltmeter Multipliers:** The ammeter shunt is in **parallel** ($R_s = R_m/(m-1)$), while the voltmeter multiplier is in **series** ($R_s = R_m(n-1)$). Reversing these is a classic blunder — verify the circuit before writing.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Units:** Always write units (A, V, W, $\Omega$, %) in the observation table. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
>
> 4. **Plotting Errors:** The calibration curve must have **$A_t$ on the x-axis** and **$A_i$ on the y-axis** (as per KTU convention). Reversing the axes is a frequent mistake.
>
> 5. **Not Tabulating CF:** Correction Factor must be tabulated and used to compute the **corrected reading** of the instrument. Skipping CF costs full marks on Part B questions.
>
> 6. **Voltmeter Loading Error:** A low-sensitivity voltmeter (e.g., $1000 \; \Omega/\text{V}$) draws significant current and alters the circuit. Always use a **high-sensitivity voltmeter** ($20{,}000 \; \Omega/\text{V}$ or higher) for accurate calibration.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Calibration of DC Ammeter, Voltmeter and Wattmeter**

**Core Definitions:**
- ✅ **Calibration** = comparing IUT with a standard of known higher accuracy.
- ✅ **True value ($A_t$)** = reading of standard instrument.
- ✅ **Indicated value ($A_i$)** = reading of instrument under test.
- ✅ **Error** $e = A_i - A_t$.
- ✅ **Correction** $c = A_t - A_i = -e$.
- ✅ **% Error** $= \dfrac{A_i - A_t}{A_t} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Correction Factor** $CF = A_t / A_i$.

**Ammeter Calibration:**
- ✅ IUT and standard ammeter connected in **series**.
- ✅ Shunt is in **parallel** with PMMC movement.
- ✅ $R_s = R_m / (m - 1)$, where $m = I/I_m$.
- ✅ PMMC = Permanent Magnet Moving Coil, linear scale, DC only.

**Voltmeter Calibration:**
- ✅ IUT and standard voltmeter connected in **parallel**.
- ✅ Multiplier is in **series** with PMMC movement.
- ✅ $R_s = R_m (n - 1)$, where $n = V/V_m$.
- ✅ Sensitivity $S_v = 1/I_{fsd}$ in $\Omega/\text{V}$.

**Wattmeter Calibration:**
- ✅ Current coil in **series** with load; potential coil in **parallel** with load.
- ✅ True power $P_t = V_t \times I_t$.
- ✅ Comparison with standard wattmeter OR computation from standard V and I.
- ✅ Power $P$ in Watts, $V$ in Volts, $I$ in Amperes.

**Practical Tips:**
- ✅ Vary the load in **5–6 equal steps** from 0 to full-scale.
- ✅ Always take readings in **increasing** AND **decreasing** order to check for **hysteresis** (if specified).
- ✅ The **calibration curve** is plotted as $A_t$ (x-axis) vs. $A_i$ (y-axis).
- ✅ The **error curve** is plotted as True value (x-axis) vs. % Error (y-axis).
- ✅ Maximum percentage error determines the **accuracy class** of the instrument.
- ✅ Standard instrument accuracy must be **at least 4× better** than the IUT.

**Deflection Equation (Bonus):**
- ✅ $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$ — shows linear scale of PMMC.

**Units Master List:**
- ✅ Current: Ampere (A); Voltage: Volt (V); Power: Watt (W); Resistance: Ohm ($\Omega$); Sensitivity: $\Omega/\text{V}$; Deflection: radian.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of resistance using DC bridges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL407 - TRANSDUCER AND MEASUREMENTS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of resistance using DC bridges # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **DC bridge** is a null-type electrical network consisting of four resistive arms, a DC excitation source, and a sensitive galvanometer/detector, used for the precise measurement of an unknown electrical resistance by the method of **comparison** against known standard resistances. The bridge is said to be "balanced" when the potential difference across the detector terminals becomes zero, indicating that no current flows through the galvanometer. At this null condition, the ratio of the known resistances in the bridge arms equals the ratio of the unknown resistance to a known reference resistance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCICL407):** The DC bridge principle is the foundational method for resistance measurement. Students must master the **Wheatstone Bridge** (for medium resistance: **1 Ω to 1 MΩ**) and the **Kelvin Double Bridge** (for low resistance: below **1 Ω**). The general classification of DC bridges for resistance measurement is: | Bridge Type | Resistance Range | Key Application | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | Wheatstone Bridge | 1 Ω to 1 MΩ | Medium resistance, general lab use | | Kelvin Double Bridge | 1 μΩ to 1 Ω | Shunts, winding resistance, contact resistance | | Megohm Bridge | > 1 MΩ | Insulation resistance, high-value resistors | | Carey-Foster Bridge | 0.001 Ω to 1 GΩ | Precision slide-wire calibration | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Balance Scale" Principle** Imagine a **two-pan balance scale** (the classic balance used in markets). You place an unknown weight (say, rice) on the **left pan** and you keep adding known standard weights (calibrated 1 kg, 500 g, 100 g pieces) on the **right pan** until the **needle in the middle points exactly to zero** (balanced). - The **rice (unknown weight)** → your **unknown resistance ($R_x$)** - The **standard weights** → your **known standard resistances ($R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$)** - The **central needle at zero** → your **galvanometer showing null deflection** Just as the balance scale tells you the unknown weight by *comparing* it with known weights, the DC bridge tells you the unknown resistance by *comparing* it with precisely known standard resistors. The galvanometer (detector) acts as the "needle" — when it reads zero, the bridge is balanced, and you can compute the unknown from the simple ratio of the known arms. > **Key Insight:** The brilliance of a bridge is that it does NOT need an accurately calibrated excitation source or an absolutely calibrated detector. It only needs **precise ratio arms**, which are far easier to manufacture to high accuracy. This is why the bridge method is one of the most accurate measurement techniques in electrical engineering. ## 1.3 Why Bridges are Used (Standard Metrics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Bridge Methods Dominate Resistance Measurement:** > - **High accuracy:** Typical accuracy is **0.1% to 0.001%** depending on the bridge. > - **No dependence on source voltage:** The null principle eliminates source stability errors. > - **Self-calibrating:** The result depends only on the ratio of known resistors. > - **Wide range:** From micro-ohms to giga-ohms with appropriate bridge variants. The fundamental physical law that governs all DC bridges is **Ohm's Law** ($V = I \cdot R$) combined with **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**. At balance, no current flows through the detector, which simplifies the network into a pure voltage divider — this is the mathematical magic that makes the bridge self-calibrating. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Wheatstone Bridge — Operational Principle The **Wheatstone Bridge** (invented by Samuel Hunter Christie in 1833 and popularized by Sir Charles Wheatstone in 1843) is the most widely used DC bridge for the measurement of medium-range resistances. ### 2.1.1 Circuit Configuration The bridge consists of: - **Four resistive arms** ($P$, $Q$, $R$, $S$) arranged in a diamond/quadrilateral shape. - **Two opposite junctions (A, C)** connected to a **DC excitation source** (battery). - **The other two opposite junctions (B, D)** connected to a **null detector** (galvanometer). By convention: - $P$ and $Q$ are called the **ratio arms** (these set the multiplication factor). - $R$ is the **standard (variable) arm** — a precision decade resistance box. - $S$ is the **unknown resistance** ($R_x$) to be measured. ### 2.1.2 Balanced Condition — The "Why" When the bridge is **balanced** (galvanometer reads zero current): - The potential at point B equals the potential at point D. - This means the voltage drop from A to B equals the voltage drop from A to D. - By KVL, the bridge splits into two independent voltage dividers across the same source voltage. > [!NOTE] > **The "How" — Step-by-Step Logic:** > 1. Current $I_1$ flows through arms $P$ and $Q$ in series. > 2. Current $I_2$ flows through arms $R$ and $S$ in series. > 3. At balance, since no current flows through the galvanometer, $I_1$ does NOT split at node B, and $I_2$ does NOT split at node D. > 4. The voltage at B (relative to C) is $V_B = I_1 \cdot Q$, and the voltage at D is $V_D = I_2 \cdot S$. > 5. At balance, $V_B = V_D$, which gives the famous **balance equation**. ### 2.1.3 Derivation of the Balance Equation Starting from $V_B = V_D$: $$I_1 \cdot Q = I_2 \cdot S$$ Also, since the same currents flow through the series arms: $$I_1 = \frac{V}{P + Q}, \quad I_2 = \frac{V}{R + S}$$ Substituting and simplifying: $$\frac{Q}{P + Q} = \frac{S}{R + S}$$ Cross-multiplying and cancelling the common terms: $$\boxed{R_x = S = R \cdot \frac{Q}{P}}$$ This is the **Wheatstone Bridge Balance Equation** — the most important formula in this entire module. ## 2.2 Sensitivity of the Wheatstone Bridge > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Bridge sensitivity analysis is frequently asked in university exams. Students must know the three types of sensitivity. **Bridge sensitivity ($S_V$)** is defined as the ratio of the change in detector output to the change in the measurand (unknown resistance) causing it. It quantifies how well the bridge responds to small unbalances. There are three distinct sensitivities: ### 2.2.1 Current Sensitivity ($S_I$) The deflection of the galvanometer per unit fractional change in the unknown resistance: $$S_I = \frac{d\theta}{dR_x / R_x}$$ ### 2.2.2 Voltage Sensitivity ($S_V$) The voltage developed across the detector terminals per unit change in the unknown resistance: $$S_V = \frac{dV_g}{dR_x}$$ ### 2.2.3 Galvanometer Current Sensitivity For a balanced bridge with a small unbalance $\Delta R_x$ in the unknown arm, the galvanometer current is: $$I_g = \frac{V \cdot \Delta R_x \cdot P}{(P+Q) \cdot [R_g(P+Q+R+S) + R(P+S) + Q(P+R)] / \text{denominator}}$$ For maximum sensitivity in a balanced bridge where all arms are equal ($P = Q = R = S = R_0$): $$I_g \approx \frac{V \cdot \Delta R}{4R \cdot (2R + R_g)}$$ > **Key Sensitivity Rules:** > 1. **Maximum sensitivity** occurs when the **galvanometer resistance equals the Thévenin resistance** seen from the detector terminals: $R_g = R_{th}$. > 2. The **ratio arms should be chosen close to unity** ($P/Q \approx 1$) for best sensitivity. > 3. Higher excitation voltage $V$ → higher sensitivity, but limited by resistor power ratings. > 4. Lower galvanometer resistance $R_g$ → generally higher current sensitivity but lower voltage sensitivity. ## 2.3 The Kelvin Double Bridge (Low Resistance Bridge) > [!CAUTION] > **Why a Special Bridge for Low Resistance?** > When measuring resistances below 1 Ω using a Wheatstone bridge, the **contact resistance** (typically 0.001 Ω to 0.01 Ω at each terminal) and the **lead resistance** become comparable to or larger than the unknown resistance. This introduces gross errors. The **Kelvin Double Bridge** (Lord Kelvin, 1865) eliminates the effect of lead and contact resistances by using a **second set of ratio arms** (called *yoke arms* or *secondary ratio arms*). ### 2.3.1 Balance Equation of the Kelvin Bridge For a Kelvin double bridge with: - $R_x$ = unknown low resistance - $R_s$ = standard low resistance - $P$, $Q$ = primary ratio arms - $p$, $q$ = secondary (yoke) ratio arms The balance equation, when the ratio arms are matched ($P/Q = p/q$), reduces to: $$R_x = R_s \cdot \frac{Q}{P}$$ The lead resistance $r$ between the unknown and standard is **completely cancelled** when $P/Q = p/q$, which is the genius of the Kelvin bridge. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master These Equations — They Appear in Almost Every KTU Exam Question on This Topic.** | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary Conditions | Standard Units | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | Wheatstone Balance Eq. | $R_x = R \cdot \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | $1\,\Omega \le R_x \le 1\,\text{M}\Omega$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 2 | Ratio Arm Product | $P \cdot S = Q \cdot R$ | At balance, cross-products equal | $\Omega^2$ | | 3 | Galvanometer Current (unbalanced) | $I_g = \dfrac{V(R \cdot Q - P \cdot S)}{\Delta}$ | $\Delta$ = bridge determinant | Amperes (A) | | 4 | Thévenin Voltage (detector) | $V_{th} = V \left( \dfrac{R}{R+S} - \dfrac{Q}{P+Q} \right)$ | Open-circuit detector voltage | Volts (V) | | 5 | Thévenin Resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{P+Q} + \dfrac{R \cdot S}{R+S}$ | Series-parallel of two pairs | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 6 | Max. Sensitivity Condition | $R_g = R_{th}$ | Galvanometer matches source | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 7 | Kelvin Bridge (balanced) | $R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | When $\dfrac{P}{Q} = \dfrac{p}{q}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 8 | Kelvin Bridge (general) | $R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{Q}{P} + \dfrac{r \cdot q}{p+q} \cdot \left( \dfrac{Q}{P} - \dfrac{q}{p} \right)$ | Full equation with lead $r$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 9 | Sensitivity Ratio | $S_b = \dfrac{E_g / E}{x / R}$ | Normalized sensitivity | Dimensionless | | 10 | Percentage Error | $\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{R_m - R_t}{R_t} \times 100$ | $R_m$ measured, $R_t$ true | Percent (%) | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!NOTE] > **Where DC Bridges are Used in Industry:** > - **Cable manufacturing:** Verifying conductor resistance of power cables. > - **Motor/transformer winding tests:** Measuring DC resistance of coils. > - **Earth resistance measurement:** Grounding system verification in substations. > - **Strain gauge calibration:** Using quarter/half bridge configurations. > - **Shunt resistor testing:** Verifying current-measuring shunts. > - **Battery internal resistance:** Quality control in cell manufacturing. > - **RTD calibration:** Precision temperature sensor verification. > - **PCB trace resistance:** Quality control in printed circuit board production. The bridge method is so fundamental that virtually every digital multimeter (DMM) and LCR meter still uses a bridge-derived principle (or its modern equivalent — the ratiometric method) to measure resistance with high accuracy. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Balance Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the MOST IMPORTANT derivation for KTU exams. Every step must be shown explicitly.** ### Step 1: Define the Circuit and Label the Currents Consider the Wheatstone bridge with four arms: $P$, $Q$, $R$, and $S$. Let the DC excitation voltage be $V$ applied across nodes A and C. The galvanometer (with internal resistance $R_g$) is connected between nodes B and D. Let: - $I_1$ = current flowing through arms $P$ and $Q$ (left branch: A → B → C) - $I_2$ = current flowing through arms $R$ and $S$ (right branch: A → D → C) - $I_g$ = current flowing through the galvanometer (B → D) By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at node B: $$I_1 = I_Q + I_g \quad \text{(current entering = current leaving)}$$ By KCL at node D: $$I_2 + I_g = I_S \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_S = I_2 + I_g$$ ### Step 2: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) — Loop ABD Going around the closed loop A → B → D → A (containing $P$, $Q$, galvanometer): $$I_1 \cdot P + I_g \cdot R_g - I_2 \cdot R = 0$$ Rearranging: $$I_1 \cdot P + I_g \cdot R_g = I_2 \cdot R \quad \cdots \text{(Equation 1)}$$ ### Step 3: Apply KVL — Loop BCD Going around the closed loop B → C → D → B (containing $Q$, $S$, galvanometer): $$I_1 \cdot Q - I_2 \cdot S - I_g \cdot R_g = 0$$ Rearranging: $$I_1 \cdot Q = I_2 \cdot S + I_g \cdot R_g \quad \cdots \text{(Equation 2)}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the Balance Condition At balance, the galvanometer reads zero deflection, which means: $$I_g = 0$$ This single condition **decouples the two branches** of the bridge. ### Step 5: Simplify with $I_g = 0$ When $I_g = 0$: From KCL at node B: $I_1 = I_Q$ From KCL at node D: $I_2 = I_S$ From Equation 1 (with $I_g = 0$): $$I_1 \cdot P = I_2 \cdot R$$ From Equation 2 (with $I_g = 0$): $$I_1 \cdot Q = I_2 \cdot S$$ ### Step 6: Divide the Two Equations Taking the ratio of Equation 1 to Equation 2: $$\frac{I_1 \cdot P}{I_1 \cdot Q} = \frac{I_2 \cdot R}{I_2 \cdot S}$$ Cancelling $I_1$ and $I_2$: $$\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S}$$ ### Step 7: Solve for the Unknown Resistance $S$ ($= R_x$) Cross-multiplying: $$P \cdot S = Q \cdot R$$ Solving for $S$: $$\boxed{S = R_x = R \cdot \frac{Q}{P}}$$ > **Alternative Form (Cross-Multiplication):** Many textbooks write this as $P \cdot R_x = Q \cdot R$, which is algebraically identical. The cross-products of the *opposite arms* of the bridge are equal at balance. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Thévenin Equivalent of the Bridge (Detector Side) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Thévenin equivalent seen from the galvanometer terminals is essential for sensitivity analysis and for understanding detector loading.** ### Step 1: Find the Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{th}$ Across B-D (with galvanometer removed) With the galvanometer removed ($I_g = 0$), the two branches become independent voltage dividers across the source $V$. The voltage at node B (with respect to node C, which is grounded) is: $$V_B = V \cdot \frac{Q}{P + Q}$$ The voltage at node D (with respect to node C) is: $$V_D = V \cdot \frac{S}{R + S}$$ The open-circuit voltage (Thévenin voltage) is: $$V_{th} = V_B - V_D = V \left( \frac{Q}{P + Q} - \frac{S}{R + S} \right)$$ ### Step 2: Find the Thévenin Resistance $R_{th}$ Set the source $V = 0$ (short-circuit the battery). Looking into terminals B-D: - The resistance from B to A to source-side-short to C and back to B through Q is: $P \parallel Q$ (arms $P$ and $Q$ are in parallel between B and C). - The resistance from D to A to short to C and back to D through S is: $R \parallel S$ (arms $R$ and $S$ are in parallel between D and C). These two parallel combinations are in series (from B to D via the shorted source): $$R_{th} = \frac{P \cdot Q}{P + Q} + \frac{R \cdot S}{R + S}$$ ### Step 3: Galvanometer Current With the galvanometer reconnected: $$I_g = \frac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_g}$$ For maximum power transfer (maximum deflection for a given small unbalance), set $R_g = R_{th}$. ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Kelvin Double Bridge Balance Equation ### Step 1: Define the Circuit Parameters The Kelvin double bridge has the unknown low resistance $R_x$ and standard low resistance $R_s$ connected by a heavy low-resistance link of resistance $r$. Two pairs of ratio arms ($P, Q$ and $p, q$) are used. ### Step 2: Apply Y-Δ or Mesh Analysis The detailed mesh analysis yields the general balance equation: $$R_x = R_s \cdot \frac{Q}{P} + \frac{r \cdot q}{p + q} \cdot \left( \frac{Q}{P} - \frac{q}{p} \right)$$ ### Step 3: The Elimination Condition The second term represents the **error introduced by the lead resistance $r$**. To make this term zero, we need: $$\frac{Q}{P} = \frac{q}{p} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{p}{q}$$ When this condition is met, the equation reduces to: $$\boxed{R_x = R_s \cdot \frac{Q}{P}}$$ This is the key advantage of the Kelvin bridge — the error term vanishes when the ratio arms are matched. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem (Typical 7-mark question):** A Wheatstone bridge has ratio arms of 100 Ω and 1000 Ω. The standard arm is a decade resistance box. When measuring an unknown resistance, balance is achieved at $R = 472 \, \Omega$. Find the unknown resistance and the percentage error if the true value is 4715 Ω. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Identify the given values. - $P = 100 \, \Omega$ (one ratio arm) - $Q = 1000 \, \Omega$ (other ratio arm) - $R = 472 \, \Omega$ (standard arm, decade box reading at balance) - $R_t = 4715 \, \Omega$ (true value given in the problem) **Step 2:** Apply the balance equation. $$R_x = R \cdot \frac{Q}{P} = 472 \times \frac{1000}{100}$$ $$R_x = 472 \times 10 = 4720 \, \Omega$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the percentage error. $$\% \text{Error} = \frac{R_x - R_t}{R_t} \times 100 = \frac{4720 - 4715}{4715} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{Error} = \frac{5}{4715} \times 100 = 0.106\%$$ **Step 4:** Conclude. > The measured value is $4720 \, \Omega$, which is in error by approximately **0.106%** relative to the true value. This is well within the typical accuracy of a standard Wheatstone bridge. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Wheatstone vs. Kelvin > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU students often confuse the two. Use this matrix to clarify.** | Parameter | Wheatstone Bridge | Kelvin Double Bridge | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Resistance Range | 1 Ω to 1 MΩ | 1 μΩ to 1 Ω | | Ratio Arms | One pair ($P$, $Q$) | Two pairs ($P$, $Q$) and ($p$, $q$) | | Error Source | Contact and lead resistance | Lead resistance cancelled by design | | Balance Equation | $R_x = R \cdot Q/P$ | $R_x = R_s \cdot Q/P$ (with $P/Q = p/q$) | | Galvanometer | Standard D'Arsonval | Standard or sensitive galvanometer | | Accuracy | ± 0.1% to ± 0.5% | ± 0.05% to ± 0.2% | | Lead Compensation | None | Automatic (by yoke arms) | | Application | General lab, medium R | Shunts, motor windings, low R | | Complexity | Simple (4 arms) | More complex (6 arms) | # 4. and estimate its approximate range"] Step2["Step 2: Select Appropriate Ratio Arms P and Q

until Galvanometer reads ZERO"] Step7["Step 7: Record Balanced R value
Compute Rx = R times Q divided by P"] Step8["Step 8: Repeat for different P/Q ratios
to verify consistency"] Step1 --> Step2 Step2 --> Step3 Step3 --> Step4 Step4 --> Step5 Step5 --> Step6 Step6 --> Step7 Step7 --> Step8 ``` ## 4.3 Kelvin Double Bridge — Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD Kx["Unknown Resistance Rx

The galvanometer connection between the midpoints of the two ratio-arm pairs automatically cancels the effect of the connecting link resistance $r$. ## 4.4 Sensitivity Analysis — Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["Is the bridge balanced
