<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State any two advantages of LVDT over a potentiometric displacement sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Frictionless and contactless operation** — the core does not touch the windings, eliminating mechanical wear and giving theoretically infinite mechanical life (1.5 marks).
2. **Infinite resolution** — since output is a continuous function of displacement, resolution is limited only by the readout instrument, not by the sensor (1 mark).
3. **Bidirectional measurement with single output** — phase information gives direction (0.5 marks, optional bonus point).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Why is the output of an LVDT zero at the null position even though two secondary windings are present?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
At the null (center) position, the ferromagnetic core is symmetrically placed so that the magnetic flux linking the upper secondary S₁ equals the flux linking the lower secondary S₂ (1.5 marks). Since the secondaries are connected in **series-opposition**, the two induced EMFs $e_{s1}$ and $e_{s2}$ are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction around the loop (1 mark). They therefore cancel each other exactly, giving a net differential output of **zero volts** (0.5 marks).

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — LVDT Focus

#### Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]
**With the help of a neat construction diagram, explain the working principle of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its output voltage.**

**Model Solution Outline:**
* Diagram of LVDT showing primary, two secondaries, and movable core (2 marks).
* Explanation of mutual induction and series-opposed connection (2 marks).
* Derivation: $e_o(t) = 2 \alpha E_0 \, x \, \sin(\omega t)$ and $E_{o,\text{rms}} = S \cdot x$ (3 marks).

**Detailed Solution:**

An LVDT consists of a **primary winding** P wound centrally on a hollow cylindrical former, and **two identical secondary windings** S₁ and S₂ placed on either side of the primary. A **soft-iron core** slides axially inside the former. The primary is excited by an AC source $e_p$, producing an alternating flux $\Phi$ along the axis.

When the core is at the **null (central) position**, equal flux links S₁ and S₂. Because the secondaries are connected in **series-opposition**, the induced EMFs are equal and opposite, giving net output:

$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 0$$

If the core is displaced by $x$ toward S₁, the flux through S₁ increases while the flux through S₂ decreases. Modeling the change linearly in the operating region:

$$\Phi_1 = \Phi_0 (1 + \alpha x), \qquad \Phi_2 = \Phi_0 (1 - \alpha x)$$

Using the transformer EMF equation $E_s = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_{\max}$ and substituting:

$$e_{s1} = E_0 (1 + \alpha x) \sin(\omega t)$$

$$e_{s2} = E_0 (1 - \alpha x) \sin(\omega t)$$

Differential output:

$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 2 \alpha E_0 \, x \, \sin(\omega t)$$

In RMS form: $E_{o,\text{rms}} = S \cdot x$, where $S = 2 \alpha E_{0,\text{rms}}$ is the sensitivity (V/mm). The **phase** of $e_o$ relative to $e_p$ indicates the **direction** of displacement.

#### Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]
**In an LVDT experiment, the following readings were recorded. Plot the characteristics and find the sensitivity and range.**

| Displacement $x$ (mm) | −5 | −3 | −1 | 0 | +1 | +3 | +5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output $E_o$ (V) | −2.45 | −1.48 | −0.50 | 0.02 | 0.51 | 1.49 | 2.46 |

**Model Solution:**

Plotting $E_o$ versus $x$ gives a straight line through the origin. Using the **two-point slope method** between $x = 0$ and $x = +5$ mm:

$$S = \dfrac{2.46 - 0.02}{5 - 0} = \dfrac{2.44}{5} = 0.488\ \text{V/mm} \approx 0.49\ \text{V/mm}$$

Similarly, between $x = 0$ and $x = -5$ mm:

$$S = \dfrac{-2.45 - 0.02}{-5 - 0} = 0.494\ \text{V/mm}$$

Average sensitivity $S \approx 0.49\ \text{V/mm}$ (3 marks).

**Range** = $\pm 5\ \text{mm}$ = **10 mm total linear range** (2 marks).

**Null voltage** = 0.02 V (residual, ideally zero) (1 mark).

**% Non-linearity** at +3 mm:
Ideal value = $0.49 \times 3 = 1.47$ V; actual = 1.49 V; error = 0.02 V; FS = 2.46 V.
$$N_L = \dfrac{0.02}{2.46} \times 100 \approx 0.81\ \%$$ (1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — RVDT Focus

#### Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]
**Explain the construction and working of an RVDT. Compare it with LVDT.**

**Model Solution Outline:**
* Construction diagram of RVDT (primary + 2 secondaries + shaped rotor) (2 marks).
* Working: rotation modulates flux linking; output $\propto \theta$ (3 marks).
* Comparison table with LVDT on 6 parameters (2 marks).

**Detailed Solution:**

An RVDT is constructed identically to an LVDT but the core is replaced by a **shaped ferromagnetic rotor** that rotates about the coil axis instead of sliding linearly. The primary is energized with a sinusoidal AC source. The rotor's geometry is designed so that in the null orientation, equal flux links the two secondaries.

When the rotor is turned by an angle $\theta$ from null, the flux coupling becomes asymmetric: one secondary sees increased flux and the other sees decreased flux. The differential output is:

$$E_{o,\text{rms}} = S_R \cdot \theta$$

where $S_R$ is sensitivity in V/°.

The phase of $E_o$ relative to the primary excitation indicates the direction of rotation. RVDTs are typically linear over $\pm 40°$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | LVDT | RVDT |
|---|---|---|
| Quantity measured | Linear displacement $x$ | Angular displacement $\theta$ |
| Core type | Sliding cylinder | Rotating shaped cam |
| Output equation | $E_o = S \cdot x$ | $E_o = S \cdot \theta$ |
| Units of sensitivity | V/mm | V/° |
| Typical range | $\pm 1$ to $\pm 500$ mm | $\pm 30°$ to $\pm 90°$ |
| Application | Hydraulic piston, strain gauge jig | Aircraft control surface, throttle valve |

#### Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]
**In an RVDT calibration, the following data is obtained. Calculate the sensitivity and the unknown angle when the output is 3.2 V.**

| Angle $\theta$ (°) | −30 | −20 | −10 | 0 | +10 | +20 | +30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output $E_o$ (V) | −3.10 | −2.05 | −1.02 | 0.05 | 1.03 | 2.06 | 3.12 |

**Model Solution:**

Plotting gives a linear characteristic. Sensitivity from $x = 0$ to $+30°$:

$$S_R = \dfrac{3.12 - 0.05}{30 - 0} = \dfrac{3.07}{30} = 0.1023\ \text{V/°}$$ (3 marks).

Verify using $-30°$ point:

$$S_R = \dfrac{-3.10 - 0.05}{-30 - 0} = 0.105\ \text{V/°}$$ (1 mark).

Average sensitivity $S_R \approx 0.104\ \text{V/°}$ (1 mark).

**Unknown angle** for $E_o = 3.2$ V:

$$\theta = \dfrac{E_o - e_{\text{null}}}{S_R} = \dfrac{3.2 - 0.05}{0.104} = \dfrac{3.15}{0.104} \approx 30.3°$$ (2 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract null voltage** when calculating displacement. Always use $x = (E_o - E_{\text{null}}) / S$, not just $E_o / S$.
> 2. **Ignoring the phase** — students often report $|x|$ and forget to mention direction (in phase vs. 180° out of phase).
> 3. **Mixing up units** — RVDT sensitivity is V/**degree**, not V/radian. KTU expects degree-based units unless specified.
> 4. **Failing to plot** — without a clearly drawn $V$ vs. $x$ (or $V$ vs. $\theta$) graph with axes labeled, you lose 2 marks even if calculations are correct.
> 5. **Writing LVDT instead of RVDT** (or vice versa) in the comparison table — copy the correct column header.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read This Before the Exam):**

* **LVDT** = Linear Variable Differential Transformer → measures **linear** displacement in mm.
* **RVDT** = Rotary Variable Differential Transformer → measures **angular** displacement in degrees.
* Both work on the principle of **mutual inductance variation** with a movable core/rotor.
* Primary is energized by **AC excitation** (typically 1–10 V rms at 1–10 kHz).
* Secondaries are connected in **series-opposition** → differential output.
* **At null position:** output = **0 V** (ideally).
* **Transfer function (linear region):**
  * LVDT: $E_{o,\text{rms}} = S_L \cdot x$
  * RVDT: $E_{o,\text{rms}} = S_R \cdot \theta$
* **Direction is encoded in phase**, not in magnitude. A **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** recovers direction.
* **Sensitivity** = slope of the linear portion of the $V$ vs. displacement curve.
* **Range** = $\pm X_{\max}$ (LVDT) or $\pm \theta_{\max}$ (RVDT) within which the response is linear.
* **Null voltage / Residual voltage** = small non-zero output at null due to winding imperfections.
* **% Non-linearity** = $\frac{E_{\text{actual}} - E_{\text{ideal}}}{E_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$.
* **Advantages:** contactless, frictionless, infinite resolution, rugged, bidirectional.
* **Disadvantages:** requires AC excitation, needs signal conditioning (PSD + filter), sensitive to stray magnetic fields.
* **Applications:** aerospace control surface feedback, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder position, CNC tooling, structural deflection monitoring.
* **In the lab record:** always tabulate readings, plot graph, mark null, compute $S$, compute % $N_L$, identify range, mention applications.
* **Examiner expects:** the word "**series-opposition**" and the phrase "**phase-sensitive demodulation**" to appear in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics (loading and unloading) of Temperature Sensors (RTD, Thermocouple, Thermistor) and comparison of different sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL407 - TRANSDUCER AND MEASUREMENTS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Characteristics (loading and unloading) of Temperature Sensors (RTD, Thermocouple, Thermistor) and comparison of different sensors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Temperature Sensors? (Formal Definition) A **Temperature Sensor** is a primary transducer that converts thermal energy (temperature) into a measurable electrical signal (resistance, voltage, or current). In industrial instrumentation, the three most widely used contact-type temperature sensors are the **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)**, the **Thermocouple (TC)**, and the **Thermistor (Thermal Resistor)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCICL407):** Temperature sensors are devices whose measurable electrical property (resistance for RTD/Thermistor, EMF for Thermocouple) varies as a definite, repeatable function of temperature, allowing the study of dynamic loading (heating) and unloading (cooling) response characteristics. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap Water" Intuition Imagine you are boiling water in three different pots on a stove: - The **RTD (PT100)** is like a **mercury thermometer** — slow, steady, very accurate, works across a wide range (like measuring body temp to oven temp). - The **Thermocouple** is like a **matchstick igniting** — extremely fast, rugged, but gives a tiny voltage you must carefully amplify (like a spark). - The **Thermistor** is like a **super-sensitive fingertip** — extremely responsive in a narrow range (say, 0–100 °C), but burns easily outside it. When you **turn ON the stove**, each pot's temperature rises (this is the **loading curve**). When you **turn OFF the stove**, each pot cools down (this is the **unloading curve**). The shapes of these curves reveal vital information about how fast, how stable, and how accurate each sensor is. > [!NOTE] > **Loading** = Sensor subjected to a step change in temperature (heating up). > **Unloading** = Sensor allowed to return to ambient (cooling down). > The **time constant (τ)** is derived from these curves and is the single most important dynamic parameter of a temperature sensor. ## 1.3 Why Study Loading and Unloading Characteristics? In a real plant (e.g., a chemical reactor, HVAC system, or oil refinery), temperature never stays constant. Engineers must know: 1. **How quickly** the sensor detects a change (response time / dynamic response). 2. **Whether the sensor returns to its original reading** when temperature returns to original (hysteresis and repeatability). 3. **Whether the response is linear** (so calibration is simple). This experiment captures all three of these properties empirically. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Standard reference temperature for RTD (PT100):** 0 °C → R = 100 Ω > - **Seebeck Coefficient (Thermocouple Type K):** ≈ 41 µV/°C (at 25 °C) > - **Thermistor B-constant (typical NTC):** 3000 K – 5000 K > - **Time Constant Definition:** Time to reach **63.2%** (i.e., 1 − e⁻¹) of final step value. > - **Thermal Time Constant (τ) = m·c/h**, where m = mass, c = specific heat, h = heat transfer coefficient. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Sensors — Operating Principles ### 2.1.1 Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) **Principle:** The electrical resistance of a pure metal increases linearly with temperature. The most common RTD is **Platinum PT100** (R = 100 Ω at 0 °C). **Why does resistance increase?** As temperature rises, metal atoms vibrate more (lattice vibrations/phonons), scattering conduction electrons more frequently, hence R ↑. **Callendar-Van Dusen Equation** (for positive temperature range): $$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 \right]$$ For temperatures below 0 °C, a fourth-order term is added: $$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100)\, T^3 \right]$$ Where: - $R_0$ = resistance at 0 °C (= 100 Ω for PT100) - $T$ = temperature in °C - $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ - $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$ - $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-4}$ **Temperature coefficient of Platinum (α):** $$\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0} = 0.00385\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}\ (\text{DIN/IEC standard})$$ ### 2.1.2 Thermocouple (TC) **Principle: Seebeck Effect** — When two dissimilar metals are joined at a "hot junction" and the other ends are held at a "cold (reference) junction", an EMF is generated that depends only on the temperature difference. **Seebeck EMF equation (empirical polynomial, ITS-90 standard):** $$E = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i \, T^i + a_0 \, e^{a_1 (T - a_2)^2}$$ Common thermocouple types: - **Type K** (Chromel-Alumel): −200 to +1260 °C, ~41 µV/°C - **Type J** (Iron-Constantan): −40 to +760 °C - **Type T** (Copper-Constantan): −200 to +370 °C - **Type E** (Chromel-Constantan): higher sensitivity (~68 µV/°C) > [!NOTE] > The output of a thermocouple is **non-linear** and requires either polynomial compensation or a look-up table (ITS-90 reference tables) to convert mV → °C. ### 2.1.3 Thermistor (NTC type) **Principle:** Thermistors are semiconductor devices (metal oxides like Mn, Ni, Co oxides sintered together) whose resistance **decreases exponentially** with temperature (NTC = Negative Temperature Coefficient). **B-parameter equation (Steinhart-Hart simplified form):** $$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$ Where: - $R_T$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin) - $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually 298.15 K = 25 °C) - $B$ = beta constant (typically 3000–5000 K), given in datasheet **Steinhart-Hart equation (more accurate, 3 coefficients):** $$\frac{1}{T} = a + b \ln R_T + c (\ln R_T)^3$$ ## 2.2 Loading and Unloading — Dynamic Response When a temperature sensor at ambient temperature $T_a$ is suddenly immersed in a bath at temperature $T_\infty$ (loading), the sensor temperature $T(t)$ follows Newton's Law of Heating/Cooling: $$T(t) = T_\infty + (T_a - T_\infty)\, e^{-t/\tau}$$ For **loading** (sensor going from cold to hot): $$T(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_a)\, e^{-t/\tau}$$ For **unloading** (sensor going from hot to cold): $$T(t) = T_a + (T_\infty - T_a)\, e^{-t/\tau}$$ Where: - $\tau = \frac{m c}{h A}$ = thermal time constant (seconds) - $m$ = mass of sensing element - $c$ = specific heat capacity - $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient - $A$ = surface area **Experimental Determination of τ:** At $t = \tau$, the sensor reaches 63.2% of the total temperature change. $$\frac{T(\tau) - T_a}{T_\infty - T_a} = 0.632$$ ## 2.3 Performance Parameters Measured from the Curves | Parameter | Definition | How Obtained | |-----------|------------|--------------| | **Sensitivity (S)** | Output change per °C input change | Slope of (Output vs T) curve: $S = \Delta V / \Delta T$ | | **Linearity** | How straight the calibration curve is | Max deviation from best-fit line / full scale × 100% | | **Time Constant (τ)** | Time to reach 63.2% of final value | From loading/unloading curve | | **Hysteresis** | Difference in reading for same T on loading vs unloading | Max vertical gap between two curves | | **Repeatability** | Closeness of successive readings | Standard deviation over multiple cycles | | **Range** | Min and max usable temperature | Sensor datasheet + experimental verification | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---------|---------|-------| | $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ | RTD resistance (positive range) | Ω | | $\alpha = (R_{100} - R_0)/(100\,R_0)$ | Temperature coefficient | °C⁻¹ | | $R_T = R_0\, e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | NTC thermistor resistance | Ω | | $E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (S_A - S_B)\, dT$ | Thermocouple Seebeck EMF | V | | $T(t) = T_\infty + (T_a - T_\infty)\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | First-order thermal response | °C | | $\tau = m c / (h A)$ | Thermal time constant | s | | $S = \Delta V / \Delta T$ | Sensitivity | V/°C or Ω/°C | | $e^{-1} = 0.632$ | 63.2% point (one time constant) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write the **units** next to the numerical values in your observation and calculation tables. The formula sheet for the End Semester Exam (ESE) will NOT be provided; memorize these. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: PT100 Resistance at an Arbitrary Temperature **Problem:** Calculate the resistance of a PT100 RTD at $T = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$. **Step 1 — Identify known quantities:** $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$, $T = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$. **Step 2 — Apply the Callendar-Van Dusen equation:** $$R_T = R_0\,[1 + A\,T + B\,T^2]$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$R_{100} = 100\,[1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(100) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(100)^2]$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate each term:** $$A \cdot T = (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(100) = 0.39083$$ $$B \cdot T^2 = (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(10{,}000) = -5.775 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 5 — Sum and compute:** $$R_{100} = 100\,[1 + 0.39083 - 0.005775] = 100 \times 1.385055$$ $$\boxed{R_{100} \approx 138.51\ \Omega}$$ This matches the **DIN/IEC 60751 standard** for platinum RTDs. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time Constant from Loading Curve **Problem:** During loading, a thermistor was at $T_a = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and was suddenly exposed to $T_\infty = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$. After $t = 8$ seconds, the thermistor read $T = 75.92\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Determine the time constant $\tau$. **Step 1 — Identify variables:** $$T_\infty = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_a = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}, \quad T(t) = 75.92\ ^\circ\text{C}, \quad t = 8\ \text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the first-order thermal response equation:** $$T(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_a)\,e^{-t/\tau}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$75.92 = 100 - (100 - 30)\,e^{-8/\tau}$$ $$75.92 = 100 - 70\,e^{-8/\tau}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential:** $$70\,e^{-8/\tau} = 100 - 75.92 = 24.08$$ $$e^{-8/\tau} = \frac{24.08}{70} = 0.344$$ **Step 5 — Take natural logarithm on both sides:** $$-\frac{8}{\tau} = \ln(0.344) = -1.067$$ $$\frac{8}{\tau} = 1.067$$ $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{8}{1.067} \approx 7.5\ \text{seconds}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: B-Constant of an NTC Thermistor **Problem:** A thermistor has $R_{25} = 10{,}000\ \Omega$ and $R_{100} = 1000\ \Omega$. Find the B-constant. **Step 1 — Convert °C to Kelvin:** $$T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15\ \text{K}$$ $$T = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15\ \text{K}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the B-equation:** $$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$ **Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right) = B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Compute the inverse temperatures:** $$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{373.15} = 2.6799 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Take the log of the resistance ratio:** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{1000}{10{,}000}\right) = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$ **Step 6 — Compute B:** $$B = \frac{-2.3026}{2.6799 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{-2.3026}{-6.741 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$\boxed{B \approx 3416\ \text{K}}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Linearity Error of an RTD (vs Ideal Line) **Problem:** PT100 at 50 °C should ideally have resistance $R_{ideal} = 100\,[1 + 0.00385 \times 50] = 119.25\ \Omega$ (using simple linear approximation). The actual measured resistance is $R_{actual} = 119.40\ \Omega$. Find the linearity error. $$\% \text{Linearity} = \frac{R_{actual} - R_{ideal}}{R_{FS}} \times 100\%$$ Where $R_{FS} = R_{100} - R_0 = 138.51 - 100 = 38.51\ \Omega$. $$\% \text{Linearity} = \frac{119.40 - 119.25}{38.51} \times 100\% = \frac{0.15}{38.51} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\% \text{Linearity Error} \approx 0.39\%}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sensors | Property | RTD (PT100) | Thermocouple (K) | Thermistor (NTC) | |----------|-------------|------------------|------------------| | **Principle** | Resistance ↑ with T | Seebeck EMF | Resistance ↓ with T (exponential) | | **Output Signal** | Resistance (Ω) | Voltage (µV–mV) | Resistance (Ω) | | **Sensitivity** | ~0.385 Ω/°C | ~41 µV/°C | ~200–500 Ω/°C (high!) | | **Range** | −200 to +850 °C | −200 to +1260 °C | −55 to +150 °C | | **Linearity** | Excellent (near-linear) | Moderate (needs polynomial) | Poor (highly non-linear) | | **Accuracy** | High (±0.1 °C) | Moderate (±1–2 °C) | Moderate (±0.5 °C) | | **Response Time (τ)** | 3–10 s (slow) | 0.1–1 s (very fast) | 1–5 s (fast) | | **Stability/Drift** | Excellent | Good | Poor (ages with time) | | **Self-heating** | Yes (low current needed) | None | Yes (critical) | | **Cost** | High | Low (per junction) | Very Low | | **Typical Use** | Lab, process industry | Furnace, engines, exhaust | Medical, HVAC, inrush protection | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Temperature Sensor Experimental Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR HS[Heat Source
Hot Water Bath / Oven] -->|Thermal Energy| SENS{Sensor Under Test} SENS -->|RTD PT100| R1[Resistance Bridge
Wheatstone / 4-wire] SENS -->|Thermocouple K| R2[Cold Junction Comp.
Amplifier AD595] SENS -->|Thermistor NTC| R3[Voltage Divider
with Reference R] R1 --> MUX[Data Acquisition
DAQ / Multimeter] R2 --> MUX R3 --> MUX MUX --> PC[PC / Recorder
Plot T vs t] REF[Reference Thermometer
Mercury / PT100 Calibrated] --> PC SENS -.->|Same Bath| REF ``` ## 4.2 Flow Chart — Experimental Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Apparatus Setup] --> B[Connect RTD, TC, Thermistor to DAQ] B --> C[Immerse all sensors in cold bath at T_amb] C --> D[Wait until stable reading reached] D --> E[Record T_amb and initial outputs] E --> F[Step 1: Switch ON heater to hot bath T_inf] F --> G[Record outputs every 5 s for 5 min] G --> H{All sensors steady?} H -->|No| G H -->|Yes| I[Record steady-state loading values] I --> J[Step 2: Switch OFF heater - Unloading] J --> K[Record outputs every 5 s until T_amb] K --> L{Temperature back to T_amb?} L -->|No| K L -->|Yes| M[Record final unloading values] M --> N[Plot T vs t for both phases] N --> O[Calculate tau, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis] O --> P[End of Experiment] ``` ## 4.3 First-Order Step Response — Loading & Unloading ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOAD[Loading Phase] L1[T at t=0 equals T_amb] --> L2[Rise begins] L2 --> L3[At t=tau: T = T_amb + 0.632 x Delta T] L3 --> L4[As t to infinity: T approaches T_inf] end subgraph UNLOAD[Unloading Phase] U1[At t=0: T = T_inf] --> U2[Decay begins] U2 --> U3[At t=tau: T = T_amb + 0.368 x Delta T] U3 --> U4[As t to infinity: T approaches T_amb] end LOAD --> UNLOAD ``` ## 4.4 Measurement Circuit — 4-Wire RTD Connection ```mermaid flowchart LR I[Constant Current Source I] --> A1[Lead 1 - Force +] I --> A2[Lead 4 - Force -] A1 --> RTD[PT100 Element] RTD --> A2 V1[Voltmeter +] --> A3[Lead 2 - Sense +] V2[Voltmeter -] --> A4[Lead 3 - Sense -] A3 --> RTD RTD --> A4 V1 & V2 --> V[High Impedance Voltmeter] ``` > [!NOTE] > **4-wire (Kelvin) connection** eliminates lead resistance error — essential for accurate RTD measurements in KTU lab experiments. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** **Define "thermal time constant" of a temperature sensor. How is it determined experimentally from the loading curve?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The thermal time constant $\tau$ is defined as the time required for a temperature sensor to reach **63.2%** of the total temperature change when subjected to a step change in temperature. It characterizes the dynamic (speed of) response of the sensor. $$\tau = \frac{m\,c}{h\,A}\ \text{(seconds)}$$ **Experimental determination:** The sensor is suddenly immersed in a bath at temperature $T_\infty$ from ambient $T_a$. The time at which the sensor output equals $T_a + 0.632\,(T_\infty - T_a)$ is recorded — this time is the time constant $\tau$. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Compare the sensitivity of RTD, Thermocouple, and Thermistor. Which has the highest sensitivity and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **RTD (PT100):** Sensitivity ≈ 0.385 Ω/°C - **Thermocouple (Type K):** Sensitivity ≈ 41 µV/°C - **NTC Thermistor:** Sensitivity ≈ 100–500 Ω/°C (varies with T) The **NTC thermistor** has the **highest sensitivity** because its resistance changes exponentially with temperature, producing very large changes in Ω per °C, especially in the low-temperature region. However, this comes at the cost of poor linearity and a narrow range. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the working principle of an RTD. Derive the Callendar-Van Dusen equation and calculate the resistance of a PT100 at 250 °C. (Assume standard constants.) **(b) [7 marks]** An NTC thermistor has $R_{25} = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $B = 3950\ \text{K}$. Determine (i) its resistance at 80 °C, (ii) the sensitivity (dR/dT) at 25 °C and at 80 °C. Comment on the variation of sensitivity with temperature. **Model Solution:** **(a) Working Principle of RTD (3 marks):** An RTD operates on the principle that the electrical resistance of a pure metal (platinum, nickel, copper) increases with temperature. The most common industrial RTD is the **Platinum PT100** with $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C. The sensing element is a wire wound on a ceramic bobbin or a thin-film deposit on a ceramic substrate, housed in a protective sheath. **Derivation of Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (2 marks):** For platinum, the resistance-temperature relationship is approximately linear in the range 0–650 °C. A second-order polynomial fit gives: $$R_T = R_0\,(1 + A T + B T^2)$$ The constants are determined empirically by calibration at 0 °C, 100 °C, and 444.6 °C (zinc point). For the **DIN/IEC 60751 standard**: $$A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}, \quad B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$$ **Calculation at 250 °C (2 marks):** $$R_{250} = 100\,[1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(250) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(250)^2]$$ $$R_{250} = 100\,[1 + 0.9771 - 0.0361]$$ $$R_{250} = 100 \times 1.9410 = \boxed{194.10\ \Omega}$$ **(b) NTC Calculations (7 marks):** **(i) Resistance at 80 °C (3 marks):** $$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$$ $$T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15\ \text{K}, \quad T_0 = 298.15\ \text{K}$$ $$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 2.8316 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -5.224 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$ $$B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \times (-5.224 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.0635$$ $$R_{80} = 5000 \cdot e^{-2.0635} = 5000 \times 0.127 = \boxed{634.8\ \Omega}$$ **(ii) Sensitivity dR/dT (4 marks):** Differentiating $R_T = R_0\, e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ with respect to T: $$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_T \cdot \left(-\frac{B}{T^2}\right) = -\frac{B\,R_T}{T^2}$$ **At 25 °C (T = 298.15 K):** $$\frac{dR}{dT}\bigg|_{25} = -\frac{3950 \times 5000}{(298.15)^2} = -\frac{19{,}750{,}000}{88{,}893} = \boxed{-222.2\ \Omega/^\circ\text{C}}$$ **At 80 °C (T = 353.15 K):** $$\frac{dR}{dT}\bigg|_{80} = -\frac{3950 \times 634.8}{(353.15)^2} = -\frac{2{,}507{,}460}{124{,}715} = \boxed{-20.1\ \Omega/^\circ\text{C}}$$ **Comment:** The magnitude of sensitivity **decreases drastically** as temperature increases (from 222 Ω/°C at 25 °C to 20 Ω/°C at 80 °C). This confirms the non-linear, exponential behavior of the NTC thermistor and its high sensitivity only in the low-temperature region. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the Seebeck effect. With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Type K thermocouple. List any three industrial applications of thermocouples. **(b) [7 marks]** In a loading experiment, a temperature sensor was at $T_a = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and was suddenly exposed to $T_\infty = 200\ ^\circ\text{C}$. The temperature readings recorded are: | Time (s) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | |----------|---|---|----|----|----|----|----| | T (°C) | 25 | 75 | 115 | 145 | 167 | 182 | 192 | (i) Show that the sensor follows a first-order response. (ii) Determine the time constant $\tau$. (iii) Calculate the temperature after 50 s. **Model Solution:** **(a) Seebeck Effect & Type K Thermocouple (7 marks):** The **Seebeck Effect** is the conversion of thermal energy into electrical energy at the junction of two dissimilar metals. When the two ends of a pair of dissimilar conductors (A and B) are maintained at different temperatures $T_h$ (hot junction) and $T_c$ (cold/reference junction), a net EMF is generated: $$E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (S_A - S_B)\, dT$$ where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the Seebeck coefficients (µV/°C) of the two metals. **Type K Construction (Chromel-Alumel):** - **Positive leg:** Chromel (90% Ni, 10% Cr) - **Negative leg:** Alumel (95% Ni, 2% Al, 2% Mn, 1% Si) - **Range:** −200 °C to +1260 °C - **Sensitivity:** ~41 µV/°C - The two wires are welded at the hot junction, with the other two ends connected to a measuring instrument. **Cold junction compensation (CJC)** is essential at the instrument end. **Three Industrial Applications:** 1. **Furnace temperature monitoring** in steel and glass industries. 2. **Exhaust gas temperature measurement** in automotive engines. 3. **Inlet/outlet temperature measurement** in boilers and heat exchangers. **(b) Loading Curve Analysis (7 marks):** **(i) Showing first-order response (2 marks):** For a first-order system: $T(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_a)\,e^{-t/\tau}$ Define $\Delta T(t) = T_\infty - T(t) = (T_\infty - T_a)\,e^{-t/\tau}$ So $\ln[\Delta T(t)] = \ln(T_\infty - T_a) - t/\tau$, which is **linear in t** with slope $-1/\tau$. Computing $\Delta T = 200 - T(t)$: | t (s) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | |-------|---|---|----|----|----|----|----| | T (°C) | 25 | 75 | 115 | 145 | 167 | 182 | 192 | | $\Delta T$ | 175 | 125 | 85 | 55 | 33 | 18 | 8 | | $\ln(\Delta T)$ | 5.16 | 4.83 | 4.44 | 4.01 | 3.50 | 2.89 | 2.08 | A plot of $\ln(\Delta T)$ vs $t$ is approximately linear (you may verify slope by least squares), confirming first-order behavior. **(ii) Determination of τ (3 marks):** Using the equation $\Delta T(t) = 175\,e^{-t/\tau}$. At $t = 10$ s, $\Delta T = 85$: $$85 = 175\,e^{-10/\tau}$$ $$e^{-10/\tau} = \frac{85}{175} = 0.4857$$ $$-\frac{10}{\tau} = \ln(0.4857) = -0.7220$$ $$\tau = \frac{10}{0.7220} \approx \boxed{13.85\ \text{s}}$$ **Verification:** At $t = \tau = 13.85$ s, $\Delta T = 175 \times e^{-1} = 175 \times 0.368 = 64.4$, so $T = 200 - 64.4 = 135.6$ °C (matches interpolation between t=10 and t=15 values of 115 and 145). ✓ **(iii) Temperature at t = 50 s (2 marks):** $$T(50) = 200 - 175 \cdot e^{-50/13.85}$$ $$= 200 - 175 \cdot e^{-3.61}$$ $$= 200 - 175 \times 0.0271$$ $$= 200 - 4.74 = \boxed{195.26\ ^\circ\text{C}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCICL407 Lab Viva & Exam:** > 1. **Forgetting the sign in dR/dT of NTC thermistor** — it is NEGATIVE (resistance decreases with T). Writing a positive value costs 1 mark. > 2. **Using °C in the exponent** of the B-equation — ALWAYS convert to **Kelvin** first. This is the most common mistake. > 3. **Not specifying "PT100" vs "PT1000"** — examiners expect the standard 100 Ω version; stating only "RTD" loses 0.5 mark. > 4. **Confusing time constant with response time** — $\tau$ is 63.2% rise time, **NOT** 100% (steady state). Many students take the time to reach steady state as $\tau$. > 5. **Writing units incorrectly** in observation tables — e.g., "T (C)" without "°" symbol, or writing "Ω/C" instead of "Ω/°C". Always use proper SI units. > 6. **In hysteresis calculation**, students often forget to take the **absolute value** of the difference between loading and unloading readings at the same temperature. > 7. **Missing cold-junction compensation** in thermocouple descriptions — examiners explicitly look for this term. > 8. **Plotting T on x-axis and t on y-axis** — convention is **t on x-axis, T on y-axis** (independent variable on X). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **RTD principle:** Resistance of pure metal **increases linearly** with T. PT100 = 100 Ω at 0 °C. - ✅ **Thermocouple principle:** **Seebeck Effect** — two dissimilar metals generate EMF proportional to ΔT. Output is in **µV to mV** range. - ✅ **Thermistor principle:** NTC type — resistance **decreases exponentially** with T. Use B-equation with **Kelvin** temperatures. - ✅ **Time constant (τ):** Time to reach **63.2%** of step change. Smaller τ = faster sensor. - ✅ **First-order thermal response:** $T(t) = T_\infty + (T_a - T_\infty)\,e^{-t/\tau}$ — applies to ALL three sensors. - ✅ **Callendar-Van Dusen:** $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ for $0 \le T \le 850\ ^\circ\text{C}$. - ✅ **Sensitivity ranking (highest to lowest):** NTC Thermistor > RTD > Thermocouple. - ✅ **Range ranking (widest to narrowest):** Thermocouple (K) > RTD (PT100) > Thermistor. - ✅ **Linearity ranking (best to worst):** RTD > Thermocouple > NTC Thermistor. - ✅ **4-wire (Kelvin) measurement** is mandatory for accurate RTD readings. - ✅ **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** is mandatory for thermocouples. - ✅ **Self-heating error** in RTD/Thermistor = $I^2 R$ heat — use minimum excitation current (typically 1 mA for PT100). - ✅ **Hysteresis** = difference in loading and unloading outputs at the same temperature. - ✅ **Loading curve** = T rising (heating) curve; **Unloading curve** = T falling (cooling) curve. - ✅ Always carry units and use proper graph paper (or software like MATLAB/Origin) for plots. - ✅ **KTU Viva Favorite Question:** "Why is a thermistor more sensitive than an RTD?" — Answer: Exponential vs. linear relationship.

topics.title: Characteristics of Synchros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL407 - TRANSDUCER AND MEASUREMENTS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Characteristics of Synchros ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Synchro** is an electromagnetic transducer that produces a correspondence between the angular position of its electrical rotor and the angular position of its mechanical rotor, or between the electrical angle of its stator and the mechanical angle of its rotor. Synchros are essentially **rotary position transducers** whose construction closely resembles a small three-phase wound-rotor induction motor, but are operated at low slip and frequently with one winding excited by a single-phase AC reference. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Significance):** A synchro is a precision rotary electromechanical device used for the **remote angular position indication and transmission** of torque/angle information from a mechanical shaft to an electrical system, acting as a coupling between mechanical and electrical domains. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine **two people holding opposite ends of a stretched rubber rope**, where one person twists their end. The twist travels along the rope and the other person’s hand rotates by exactly the same amount — even though there is **no physical shaft** connecting them. A synchro pair behaves in the same way: - The **transmitter** is the person who twists (input shaft). - The **receiver** is the person whose hand follows the twist (output shaft). - The **three wires** between them act like the rubber rope, carrying the angular information electrically. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme lab, the experiment focuses on plotting the **input voltage vs. output voltage characteristic**, the **electrical error vs. angular displacement characteristic**, and verifying the **transmitter–control transformer (T–CX)** and **transmitter–receiver (TX–TR)** coupling of a synchro pair. ### 1.3 Classification of Synchros (Standard KTU Taxonomy) | Synchro Type | Function | Output Quantity | |---|---|---| | **Torque Transmitter (TX)** | Sends angle to remote receiver | 3-phase stator voltages | | **Torque Receiver (TR)** | Reproduces mechanical angle | Mechanical shaft position | | **Control Transmitter (CX)** | Precision angle sensing | High-impedance stator voltages | | **Control Transformer (CT)** | Error-sensing detector | Error voltage proportional to $\Delta\theta$ | | **Differential Synchro (TDX / TDR)** | Adds/subtracts shaft angles | Mechanical sum/difference | ### 1.4 Standard Construction Features - **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with a **distributed three-phase winding** placed in slots at 120° electrical spacing. - **Rotor:** Salient-pole or cylindrical construction carrying a **single-phase concentrated winding** excited by an AC reference (typically **400 Hz**, **50–115 V RMS** in avionics; **50 Hz**, **110 V** in industrial systems). - **Slip Rings & Brushes:** Used only on the rotor side; in some designs the rotor carries the 3-phase winding and the stator carries the single-phase winding. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference:** A synchro operating at **400 Hz** is the most common in instrumentation textbooks, while **50 Hz** synchros are used in industrial-grade KTU laboratories. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle (Logical Step Breakdown) A synchro transmitter and a control transformer are connected in a standard **T–CT pair** to convert mechanical angle $\theta_T$ into an electrical error voltage. - **Step 1 — Single-Phase Excitation of the Rotor:** The rotor of the transmitter is energized by a single-phase AC reference voltage $V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. This produces a sinusoidally time-varying flux $\phi$ that links the three stator windings. - **Step 2 — Flux Distribution & Coupling:** Because the flux density in the air gap is **sinusoidally distributed in space**, the flux linking any stator phase is a function of $\cos(\theta)$ of the rotor position. - **Step 3 — Induced Stator EMFs:** Each of the three stator phases of the transmitter (S1, S2, S3) therefore produces an induced EMF with magnitude dependent on the cosine of the shaft angle. - **Step 4 — Interconnection to Control Transformer:** The three stator terminals of the TX are wired to the three stator terminals of the CT in a 1-to-1 fashion (S1–S1, S2–S2, S3–S3). - **Step 5 — Resultant CT Rotor EMF:** A current circulates in the loop formed by the TX and CT stator windings, producing a counter-flux in the CT that is displaced from the CT rotor axis. The net flux induces a residual error voltage in the CT rotor winding. - **Step 6 — Error Voltage as Position Indicator:** The error voltage $V_e$ is a sinusoidal function of the angular difference $\Delta\theta = \theta_T - \theta_{CT}$. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Lab Records & ESE (Module 2)** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{S1} = K V_R \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$ | Induced EMF in stator phase S1 | Volts (V) | | 2 | $V_{S2} = K V_R \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°)$ | EMF in S2 | Volts (V) | | 3 | $V_{S3} = K V_R \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta + 120°)$ | EMF in S3 | Volts (V) | | 4 | $V_{CT} = V_{max} \sin(\Delta\theta)$ | CT rotor output voltage | Volts (V) | | 5 | $V_{max} = K V_R \cos(\omega t)$ | Peak error voltage | Volts (V) | | 6 | $\Delta\theta = \theta_T - \theta_{CT}$ | Angular error | Degrees or rad | | 7 | $V_{R} = V_{ref} \sin(\omega t)$ | Reference excitation | Volts (V) | | 8 | $E = \frac{V_{max}}{K_{sens}}$ | Electrical error at null | Degrees | | 9 | $K_{sens} = \frac{d V_{CT}}{d \theta} \big\vert_{\theta = 90°}$ | Sensitivity at electrical null | V/degree | > **Notation Note:** $\vert x \vert$ has been deliberately replaced by $\vert \cdot \vert$ wherever an absolute magnitude is required, to comply with the KTU markdown rendering rule. Use the single vertical bar **only inside a LaTeX environment**, never inside a markdown table cell. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aircraft Remote Indicators:** Used in older Boeing/Airbus instrumentation to transmit flap, rudder, and engine-turbine angles to the cockpit display. - **Industrial Control Loops:** Heavy machinery uses 50 Hz torque synchros for **valve actuator positioning** in power plants. - **Robotics & CNC:** Modern digital equivalents (resolver, encoder) inherit their mathematical model directly from the synchro. - **Servo Follow-up Systems:** Closed-loop position control of radar antennas and gun turrets. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the Lab Characteristic Matters:** The slope of the **$V_{CT}$ vs. $\theta$** curve around the null point (90°) gives the **voltage gradient** — the single most important parameter to verify in any KTU lab viva question on synchros. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Induced EMF in Stator Phases of a Synchro Transmitter **Given:** A single-phase rotor winding is excited by $V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. The three stator phases are physically displaced at 120° electrical. **Step 1 — Time-Varying Rotor Flux** The rotor MMF produces a sinusoidal flux along its own magnetic axis. The instantaneous flux is: $$ \phi(t) = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t) $$ **Step 2 — Flux Linking Each Stator Phase** Let the rotor axis make an angle $\theta$ with respect to the S1 stator phase axis. By the spatial cosine coupling rule: $$ \phi_{S1} = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta) $$ $$ \phi_{S2} = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°) $$ $$ \phi_{S3} = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta + 120°) $$ **Step 3 — Apply Faraday’s Law of Electromagnetic Induction** By Faraday’s law, the induced EMF in any winding is the time derivative of flux linkage (with N turns): $$ V_{S1} = -N \frac{d \phi_{S1}}{d t} = -N \frac{d}{d t}\big[\phi_{max} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)\big] $$ **Step 4 — Differentiate with Respect to Time** Since $\theta$ is the slow mechanical angle and $\omega$ is the fast electrical angular frequency: $$ V_{S1} = -N \phi_{max} \omega \cos(\omega t) \cos(\theta) $$ **Step 5 — Express in the Standard Transmitter Form** Define the electrical constant $K = N \phi_{max} \omega$. Then: $$ V_{S1} = K \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta) $$ (Here, a 90° phase shift is applied by convention so the result matches the standard synchro equation form.) By identical reasoning applied to the other two stator phases: $$ V_{S2} = K \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°) $$ $$ V_{S3} = K \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta + 120°) $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Control Transformer Error Voltage **Given:** A T–CT pair with the transmitter rotor at angle $\theta_T$ and the control transformer rotor mechanically pre-positioned at $\theta_{CT} = 90° + \Delta\theta$. **Step 1 — Net Current in the Stator Loop** Connecting the two stators causes circulating currents $i_{S1}, i_{S2}, i_{S3}$ proportional to the difference in induced EMFs. The net flux in the CT air gap is a sinusoid in space whose axis lies at angle $\theta_T$. **Step 2 — Flux Coupling with the CT Rotor** The CT rotor is wound along an axis at $\theta_{CT}$. The flux linking the CT rotor is: $$ \phi_{CT} = \phi_{net} \cos(\theta_T - \theta_{CT}) $$ **Step 3 — Apply Faraday’s Law on the CT Rotor** $$ V_{CT}(t) = -N_{CT} \frac{d \phi_{CT}}{d t} $$ Assuming $\phi_{net}$ varies sinusoidally as $\phi_{net,max} \sin(\omega t)$: $$ V_{CT}(t) = -N_{CT} \phi_{net,max} \omega \cos(\omega t) \cos(\theta_T - \theta_{CT}) $$ **Step 4 — Define the Error Angle** By the **electrical-null convention**, the CT rotor is physically pre-set such that the null occurs at $\theta_{CT} = 90°$ when $\theta_T = 0°$. Letting $\Delta\theta = \theta_T - \theta_{CT}$: $$ V_{CT}(t) = V_{max} \sin(\Delta\theta) $$ where $V_{max} = N_{CT} \phi_{net,max} \omega \cos(\omega t)$ is the peak rotor voltage when the rotors are perfectly perpendicular. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Result:** The CT error voltage is **maximum at $\Delta\theta = 90°$** and **zero at $\Delta\theta = 0°$ or $180°$**. This null point is the operational sweet spot for servo feedback. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Modes of a Synchro Pair | Mode | TX Rotor | TR/CT Rotor | Stator Connection | Output Quantity | KTU Lab Test Point | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Torque Mode (TX–TR) | Energized | Free to rotate | S1–S1, S2–S2, S3–S3 | Mechanical follow-up | Verify angular equality at 0°, 30°, 60°, 90° | | Control Mode (TX–CT) | Energized | Mechanically clamped | S1–S1, S2–S2, S3–S3 | AC error voltage | Plot $V_{CT}$ vs. $\theta_T$ | | Differential Mode | Energized + Differential | Follows sum/difference | 6-wire hookup | Mechanical sum | Verify $\theta_{out} = \theta_1 \pm \theta_2$ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a T–CT Pair ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Reference Supply
400 Hz 115 V] --> B[Synchro Transmitter Rotor
Single Phase Winding] B -- Mutual Inductance --> C[Stator Windings S1 S2 S3
Three Phase 120 Degree Spaced] C -- 3 Wire Link --> D[Control Transformer Stator
S1 S2 S3] D -- Counter Flux --> E[Control Transformer Rotor
Mechanically Pre Set at 90 Degree] E --> F[Error Voltage Vct
Vac Output] F --> G[Servo Amplifier
KTU Lab Display] G -- Drives --> H[Servo Motor
Mechanical Follow Up] H -- Feedback Shaft --> E ``` ### 4.2 Stator–Rotor Winding Spatial Layout ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Stator_Slot_Topology S1[Stator Phase S1
Axis at 0 Degree]:::stator S2[Stator Phase S2
Axis at Minus 120 Degree]:::stator S3[Stator Phase S3
Axis at Plus 120 Degree]:::stator R[Rotor Winding
Single Phase at Theta]:::rotor end S1 --- S2 S2 --- S3 S3 --- S1 R -.- S1 R -.- S2 R -.- S3 classDef stator fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef rotor fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#996600,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Experimental Setup Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD Power[230 V 50 Hz Mains Supply] --> Variac[Variac 0 to 270 V AC] Variac --> StepUp[Step Up Transformer to 115 V] StepUp --> FreqConv[Frequency Converter to 400 Hz] FreqConv --> TXRotor[Synchro TX Rotor Terminals R1 R2] TXRotor --> TXStator[Synchro TX Stator S1 S2 S3] TXStator --> ThreeWire[Three Wire Interconnecting Cable] ThreeWire --> CTStator[Synchro CT Stator S1 S2 S3] CTStator --> CTRotor[Synchro CT Rotor R1 R2] CTRotor --> DMM1[Digital Multimeter 1 for Vct] CTRotor --> CRO[CRO for Waveform] TXRotor --> DMM2[Digital Multimeter 2 for Vref] Shaft[Shaft Encoder / Protractor] --> TXRotor ``` ### 4.4 Expected Characteristic Curves | Curve Plotted | X-Axis | Y-Axis | Shape | KTU Exam Hook | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Null Curve** | $\theta_T$ (deg) | $V_{CT}$ (V) | **Sine wave**, zero at 0° and 180° | Maximum marks for slope around 90° | | **Sensitivity Curve** | $\theta_T$ (deg) | $dV_{CT}/d\theta$ | **Cosine wave**, peak at 0° and 180° | Used for servo-amp design | | **Torque Curve (TX–TR)** | $\theta_T - \theta_{TR}$ (deg) | Torque (N·m) | Sinusoidal restoring torque | Demonstrates follow-up accuracy | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The null-curve experiment is the single most widely asked **14-mark question** in KTU 2024 ESE because it directly tests a student’s ability to wire, rotate, and interpret the system output. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Pattern]** **Q1. Define a synchro. Mention any two applications.** *(3 Marks – CO1, Remember Level)* **Model Answer:** A synchro is an **electromagnetic transducer** used for the remote transmission of angular position between a mechanical input shaft and an electrical (or remote mechanical) output. It is constructed similarly to a small wound-rotor induction machine with a single-phase rotor and a three-phase stator, or vice versa. *Applications:* 1. Remote angular position indication in aircraft instrumentation. 2. Servo follow-up systems for radar antenna positioning. --- **Q2. State the function of a Control Transformer (CT) in a synchro pair.** *(3 Marks – CO2, Understand Level)* **Model Answer:** A Control Transformer (CT) acts as an **error-sensing device**. Its stator is energized from a synchro transmitter, and its rotor produces an AC error voltage $V_{CT}$ that is a sinusoidal function of the angular difference between the transmitter shaft and the CT rotor. The CT itself is not free to rotate; instead, its output is fed to a servo amplifier that drives a follow-up motor to null the error. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > **ESE Module 2 – Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024)** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Synchro Transmitter–Control Transformer (T–CT) pair**. Derive the expression for the induced EMF in the stator windings. *(7 Marks – CO2, Apply Level)* **(b)** Sketch the **$V_{CT}$ vs. $\theta$ characteristic** of a CT and explain the significance of the **electrical null** and **voltage gradient**. *(7 Marks – CO3, Analyze Level)* --- **Model Solution for Q A (a):** **Construction:** A synchro TX has a **single-phase rotor** wound on a laminated rotor core with two slip rings. The **stator carries a balanced three-phase winding** distributed in slots at 120° electrical spacing. The synchro CT is similarly built but its rotor is pre-aligned such that its magnetic axis is perpendicular to the stator winding axis at the null position. **Working Principle:** 1. The TX rotor is excited by a single-phase AC reference $V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. 2. A sinusoidally distributed alternating flux is established in the air gap. 3. The flux linking each stator phase is proportional to $\cos(\theta + \text{phase offset})$. 4. By Faraday’s law, the induced EMFs in the three stator phases are: $$V_{S1} = K \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$$ $$V_{S2} = K \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°)$$ $$V_{S3} = K \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta + 120°)$$ 5. These EMFs drive circulating currents through the CT stator windings. 6. The CT produces a counter-flux; the resultant flux links the CT rotor, inducing an error voltage. **Derivation Outline (as shown in Section 3.1):** Start with $\phi(t) = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$, multiply by $\cos(\theta)$ for spatial coupling, apply Faraday’s law, and obtain the standard form. --- **Model Solution for Q A (b):** **Sketch Description:** | $\theta_T$ (deg) | 0 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $V_{CT}$ (V) | 0 | 0.5 $V_{max}$ | 0.866 $V_{max}$ | $V_{max}$ | 0.866 $V_{max}$ | 0.5 $V_{max}$ | 0 | The curve is a **sine wave** starting from zero at $\theta_T = 0°$, rising to $V_{max}$ at $\theta_T = 90°$, and falling back to zero at $\theta_T = 180°$. **Electrical Null:** The two points ($\theta_T = 0°, 180°$) where $V_{CT} = 0$. At these points, the CT rotor magnetic axis is perpendicular to the resultant air-gap flux — no flux links the rotor, so no EMF is induced. **Voltage Gradient:** The slope of the curve at the null point is $\frac{dV_{CT}}{d\theta} = V_{max} \cos(\theta_T)$. At $\theta_T = 0°$, this equals $V_{max}$ V/rad, which represents the **maximum sensitivity** of the system to small angular errors — a critical parameter for servo-amplifier design. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** Describe the construction of a **torque synchro transmitter–receiver (TX–TR) pair**. Explain how the receiver shaft automatically follows the transmitter shaft without any physical coupling. *(7 Marks – CO2, Understand Level)* **(b)** With a neat connection diagram, explain the operation of a **differential synchro** and derive the expression $\theta_{out} = \theta_1 - \theta_2$. *(7 Marks – CO3, Apply Level)* --- **Model Solution for Q B (a):** A TX–TR pair consists of two identical synchros. Both have **single-phase rotors** (excited by the same AC reference) and **three-phase stators** that are interconnected through three wires (S1–S1, S2–S2, S3–S3). When the TX rotor is at angle $\theta_1$, it induces EMFs in its stator. The TR stator receives these EMFs and produces a current distribution that creates a torque on the TR rotor. This torque is proportional to $\sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$, where $\theta_2$ is the TR rotor angle. Equilibrium ($\theta_1 = \theta_2$) is reached when the torque is zero, and the TR shaft aligns itself with the TX shaft. Hence, the **receiver shaft follows the transmitter shaft**. --- **Model Solution for Q B (b):** A **differential synchro** has a **three-phase rotor** (instead of single-phase) and a three-phase stator. It is connected between a TX and a TR such that: - The TX stator is connected to the differential stator. - The differential rotor is mechanically driven by the second input shaft at $\theta_2$. - The differential rotor is electrically connected to the TR stator. The flux axis at the differential stator is $\theta_1$, and the differential rotor superimposes a $\theta_2$ rotation, giving a resultant flux axis at the TR stator of $\theta_1 - \theta_2$. The TR shaft therefore aligns at: $$ \theta_{out} = \theta_1 - \theta_2 $$ This makes the differential synchro an **electrical subtractor** of mechanical angles. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing TX and CT rotor EMF equations** — The CT output is a **sine** function, not a cosine. Miswriting the sign costs **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Skipping the Faraday’s law step** — Examiners award marks for the explicit use of $V = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$. > 3. **Omitting the spatial-cosine coupling assumption** — Without stating that the air-gap flux is sinusoidally distributed, the derivation is incomplete. > 4. **Forgetting units** — Always mention **Volts, degrees, Hz** in the final answer. > 5. **Plotting the wrong null position** — In a CT, the **electrical null is at 90°**, not 0°. Many students incorrectly state 0°. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024** - **Synchro = Rotary transformer pair** that transmits angular information electrically. - **Two main configurations:** Torque (TX–TR) for mechanical follow-up, Control (TX–CT) for error voltage. - **Rotor:** Single-phase, AC-excited, 400 Hz (avionics) or 50 Hz (industrial). - **Stator:** Three-phase, 120° electrical spacing. - **Stator EMF equations:** $V_{Si} = K V_R \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta \pm 120° \cdot i)$. - **CT Output:** $V_{CT} = V_{max} \sin(\Delta\theta)$. - **Electrical Null:** At $\theta_T = 0°$ and $180°$, $V_{CT} = 0$. - **Voltage Gradient:** Maximum sensitivity occurs at the null point, equals $V_{max}$ V/rad. - **Differential Synchro:** Performs electrical subtraction of two mechanical angles: $\theta_{out} = \theta_1 - \theta_2$. - **Lab Verification:** Plot the null curve, the sensitivity curve, and the torque curve. - **Standard Excitation:** 115 V RMS, 400 Hz (most KTU lab kits). - **Faraday’s Law is the only tool required** to derive every equation in this module. - **KTU Bloom’s Level:** Questions are framed at *Apply* and *Analyze* levels; derivations are mandatory. - **Examiner’s Favourite:** Deriving $V_{CT} = V_{max} \sin(\Delta\theta)$ from Faraday’s law. ---

topics.title: Familiarisation of Virtual Instrumentation software (e.g., LabVIEW): Loops and charts, Case and sequence structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL407 - TRANSDUCER AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of Virtual Instrumentation software (e.g., LabVIEW): Loops and charts, Case and sequence structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Familiarisation of LabVIEW – Loops, Charts, Case & Sequence Structures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Virtual Instrumentation – Formal Definition

**Virtual Instrumentation (VI)** is a software-centric approach to measurement and automation in which a general-purpose computing platform (PC) is combined with modular hardware (DAQ cards, sensors, actuators) and graphical programming software (such as **LabVIEW** – *Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench* by National Instruments) to build user-defined test, measurement, and control systems.

A *Virtual Instrument* (also called a **VI** – the file extension and the unit of program) replaces the physical knobs, switches, and dials of a traditional bench-top instrument with on-screen **Front Panel** controls and indicators, while its **Block Diagram** acts as the graphical source code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LabVIEW uses a *Dataflow Programming Model*. A node executes **only when all its input terminals have received data** — there is no implicit sequential line-by-line execution as in text-based languages (C, Python).

### 1.2 The Three Core Windows of a LabVIEW VI

| Window | Purpose | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Front Panel** | User Interface (Controls = Inputs, Indicators = Outputs) | The dashboard of a car – steering wheel, speedometer, fuel gauge |
| **Block Diagram** | Graphical source code (wires, nodes, structures) | The engine and wiring underneath the dashboard |
| **Icon & Connector Pane** | Allows a VI to be used as a sub-VI (like a function call) | The plug and socket of a household appliance |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – The "Visual Chef"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine cooking pasta in a graphical way. You do not write a recipe as text — instead, you place a *Boil Water* block, connect it via an arrow (wire) to a *Cook Pasta* block, and finally pipe the result to a *Serve* block. The arrow (wire) carries the actual spaghetti (data). If the water is not yet boiling, the *Cook Pasta* block simply waits — this is the **dataflow paradigm** of LabVIEW.

### 1.4 Standard Terminology Used in the Experiment

- **Terminals** – The points on a node where data enters or leaves.
- **Wires** – Paths along which data flows. Wire thickness and colour indicate data type (blue = integer, orange = floating-point, green = boolean, pink = string).
- **Tunnels** – The points where data enters or leaves a Structure (Loop, Case, Sequence).
- **Auto-Indexing** – A tunnel feature of Loops that automatically builds an array from loop iterations.
- **Iteration Terminal (i)** – A built-in numeric terminal inside a For/While Loop that gives the current iteration count starting from **0**.
- **Shift Registers** – Variables that carry data from one loop iteration to the next.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Declaration for KTU Records:** LabVIEW files are saved with the extension **`.vi`**, and projects are saved as **`.lvproj`**. The numeric constants and metrics used in this experiment are: iteration start index = **0**, default chart update rate = **millisecond range**, and the time-base for waveform charts = **seconds** unless otherwise specified by the user.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Loop Structures in LabVIEW

LabVIEW provides exactly **two** loop structures, located on the **Functions Palette → Programming → Structures**.

### 2.1.1 For Loop

A **For Loop** executes its sub-diagram a **pre-determined number of times**, denoted by the value wired to the count terminal **N** (top-left corner of the loop border).

**Why it exists:** To perform a known, bounded number of iterations — e.g., acquire 1000 samples, average 50 readings, generate a 256-point sine wave.

**How it works (logic steps):**
1. Reads the value at the count terminal **N** *once* at the start.
2. Initialises the **iteration terminal (i)** to **0**.
3. Executes the sub-diagram.
4. Increments **i** by **1**.
5. Repeats steps 3–4 until **i = N − 1**, then exits.
6. Total iterations executed = **N**.

### 2.1.2 While Loop

A **While Loop** executes its sub-diagram **until a boolean stop condition becomes TRUE**.

**Why it exists:** For indefinite, event-driven or continuous operations — e.g., continuously monitor a temperature until an alarm threshold is exceeded.

**How it works (logic steps):**
1. Evaluates the **conditional terminal (stop sign)**.
2. If `FALSE` → executes the sub-diagram.
3. Increments **iteration terminal (i)** by **1**.
4. Returns to step 1.
5. If `TRUE` → exits the loop.
6. Minimum guaranteed iterations = **1** (loop body executes *before* stop is checked).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Board-Exam Favourite):**
> - *For Loop* → known, finite count → used for **data generation / array processing**.
> - *While Loop* → unknown count, condition-based → used for **real-time continuous monitoring**.

### 2.2 The Two Charting Tools

| Feature | **Waveform Chart** | **XY Graph** |
|---|---|---|
| X-axis | Time (auto-generated, scrolling) | User-supplied array (X) |
| Y-axis | Data points appended in real time | User-supplied array (Y) |
| Data Input | Single points (one at a time) **or** an array | **Must** be two arrays (X and Y) |
| Memory Model | Internal **buffer** (history length) | No buffer – plots array on each call |
| Typical Use | Live streaming sensor data | Lissajous figures, calibrated plots |

### 2.3 The Two Decision / Execution Structures

| Structure | Programming Analogue | Execution Order |
|---|---|---|
| **Case Structure** | `if … then … else` / `switch … case` | Executes **only one** case at a time, selected by a Boolean, Integer, String, or Enum selector |
| **Flat Sequence Structure** | Sequential statements | Executes frames **left → right**, top → bottom |
| **Stacked Sequence Structure** | Sub-routine in a state machine | Executes frame 0, then 1, … then n; carries local variables between frames |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** A *Stacked Sequence Structure* is **deprecated** by NI for new designs (since LabVIEW 7.0). Modern KTU answers should prefer the **Flat Sequence Structure** and **State Machine architecture** using While + Case. However, knowing the stacked form is still required for legacy lab VIs.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | For Loop iteration count | $N = \text{value at count terminal}$ | Dimensionless integer |
| 2 | While Loop stop logic | $\text{Stop} = \text{TRUE} \Rightarrow \text{exit}$ | Boolean |
| 3 | Iteration index (both loops) | $i \in \{0,\ 1,\ 2,\ \dots,\ N-1\}$ | Starts at **0** (not 1) |
| 4 | Auto-Indexing output array size | $\vert \text{Array}_{\text{out}} \vert = N$ | Length of array = number of iterations |
| 5 | Waveform Chart update period | $T_{s} = \dfrac{1}{f_{s}}$ | **seconds** |
| 6 | Sampling frequency (Nyquist safe) | $f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max,signal}}$ | **Hz** |
| 7 | Chart History Length | $H = \text{default} = 1024$ | Points retained on screen |
| 8 | Waveform generation (sine) | $y(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$ | $A$ = amplitude, $f$ = **Hz**, $\phi$ = rad |
| 9 | Case Structure selector type | Boolean $\vert$ Int $\vert$ String $\vert$ Enum | Each frame is labelled accordingly |
| 10 | Flat Sequence frame order | Frame $0 \rightarrow$ Frame $1 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow$ Frame $n$ | Strictly sequential |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Loops** are the backbone of every real-time DAQ (Data Acquisition) program in industry — vibration monitoring, ECG acquisition, automotive ECU testing.
- **Waveform Charts** mimic the *strip-chart recorders* historically used in process plants.
- **XY Graphs** are used in *Lissajous* and *phase-plane* analysis in control systems.
- **Case Structures** implement FSMs (Finite State Machines) — the standard architecture for automated test stands in NI-certified industries.
- **Sequence Structures** are essential when the **order** of operations (e.g., Open → Configure → Acquire → Close) is non-negotiable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Auto-Indexing in a For Loop

The most common analytical task in this experiment is to generate a 1-D waveform of length **N** using a For Loop with auto-indexing enabled. We derive the resulting array mathematically.

**Given:**
- Count terminal value: $N$
- Body of the loop produces a scalar value $y_i$ at iteration $i$

**Step 1 — Identify the iteration index range.**

By definition of a For Loop in LabVIEW, the iteration terminal outputs integers from 0 upwards.

$$i \in \{0,\ 1,\ 2,\ \dots,\ N-1\}$$

**Step 2 — Write the scalar output at each iteration.**

Assume the loop body computes a sampled sinusoid:

$$y_i = A \cdot \sin\!\left(2\pi f \cdot i \cdot T_{s} + \phi\right)$$

where $T_{s} = \dfrac{1}{f_{s}}$ is the sampling interval (in **seconds**) and $f$ is the signal frequency in **Hz**.

**Step 3 — Substitute $T_{s}$ to express $y_i$ in canonical form.**

$$y_i = A \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi f \cdot i}{f_{s}} + \phi\right)$$

**Step 4 — Enable auto-indexing on the output tunnel.**

When auto-indexing is **enabled** on the right-edge tunnel of a For Loop, the tunnel automatically **collects** every $y_i$ into a 1-D array. The size of the resulting array equals the number of loop iterations.

$$\text{Array}\ Y = \bigl[\ y_0,\ y_1,\ y_2,\ \dots,\ y_{N-1}\ \bigr]$$

$$\bigl\vert Y \bigr\vert = N$$

**Step 5 — Verify with a worked numerical example (KTU lab style).**

Let $A = 5$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$, $f_{s} = 1000\ \text{Hz}$, $\phi = 0$, $N = 10$.

Then $T_{s} = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 0.001\ \text{s}$.

Compute $y_i$ for $i = 0$ to $9$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_0 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 0}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(0) = 0 \\
y_1 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(0.3142) \approx 1.545 \\
y_2 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 2}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(0.6283) \approx 2.939 \\
y_3 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 3}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(0.9425) \approx 4.045 \\
y_4 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 4}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(1.2566) \approx 4.755 \\
y_5 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 5}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(1.5708) = 5.000 \\
y_6 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 6}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(1.8850) \approx 4.755 \\
y_7 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 7}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(2.1991) \approx 4.045 \\
y_8 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 8}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(2.5133) \approx 2.939 \\
y_9 &= 5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 9}{1000}\right) = 5 \cdot \sin(2.8274) \approx 1.545
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final array after auto-indexing.**

$$Y = [\,0,\ 1.545,\ 2.939,\ 4.045,\ 4.755,\ 5.000,\ 4.755,\ 4.045,\ 2.939,\ 1.545\,]$$

$$\vert Y \vert = 10 = N \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Structure Selection for Common Lab Tasks

| Lab Task | Best Structure | Justification (Logical Steps) |
|---|---|---|
| Generate a fixed-length sine wave | **For Loop** | Iteration count is known ($N = 256$, $512$, $1024$, etc.) |
| Continuously read a thermocouple | **While Loop** | Stop only when user presses STOP button |
| Plot live temperature vs. time | **While Loop + Waveform Chart** | New data point added each iteration; chart handles buffering |
| Plot Lissajous figure (Ch1 vs. Ch2) | **For Loop + XY Graph** | Need pairs (X, Y); XY Graph accepts two arrays |
| Choose waveform shape (sine/square/triangle) | **Case Structure** | Selector chooses one of three cases |
| Acquire → Save → Plot in strict order | **Flat Sequence** | Operations must be sequential, not parallel |
| Build a multi-state test sequencer | **Stacked Sequence or State Machine** | Each step depends on the previous state |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Explanation of the Dataflow Execution Rule

To **prove** the dataflow rule, consider three nodes **A**, **B**, **C** in a block diagram where:

- **B** depends on output of **A** (wired together).
- **C** depends on output of **B** (wired together).

**Step 1 — Identify inputs of A.** A has no upstream inputs, so it executes immediately.

$$A \text{ executes at } t = t_0$$

**Step 2 — Identify inputs of B.** B requires A's output. B cannot start until the wire carries a valid value from A.

$$B \text{ executes at } t = t_0 + \Delta t_A \quad \text{where } \Delta t_A \text{ is A's execution time}$$

**Step 3 — Identify inputs of C.** C requires B's output.

$$C \text{ executes at } t = t_0 + \Delta t_A + \Delta t_B$$

**Step 4 — General Rule.**

$$\text{Node } X \text{ executes when } \forall \text{ inputs} \in \{\text{upstream nodes of } X\}, \text{ the wire value is available.}$$

This is the **fundamental** reason why a *Sequence Structure* is sometimes required — to force ordering when the dataflow rule alone cannot guarantee it (e.g., two independent initialisation blocks that must run in a particular order).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

```mermaid
    G --> H["STOP"]

    style F fill:#FFD580,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```
