# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Differentiate between a **For Loop** and a **While Loop** in LabVIEW. State one engineering application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | For Loop | While Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Iteration Count | Fixed (wired to **N** terminal) | Variable (depends on stop condition) |
| Stopping Criterion | Executes exactly **N** times | Executes until the **stop** Boolean is **TRUE** |
| Iteration Index Starts At | **0** | **0** |
| Default Use | Generating fixed-length arrays | Real-time continuous monitoring |
| Application | Generate a 1024-point FFT buffer | Continuously log thermocouple temperature to file |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
What is the difference between a **Waveform Chart** and an **XY Graph** in LabVIEW? When is each preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **Waveform Chart** displays data as it is generated (one point or array at a time) against an *auto-generated time axis*. It has a **buffer** and supports scrolling history (default history = **1024** points). Preferred for *real-time streaming data*, e.g., a live temperature display.
- An **XY Graph** requires two user-supplied arrays (X and Y) and plots them against each other. It has *no buffer*. Preferred for *multi-variable relationships* like Lissajous figures, calibration curves, or phase portraits.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOSEN: While Loop + Waveform Chart**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCICL407]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled **Block Diagram** and **Front Panel**, explain how a **While Loop** can be used to continuously display a real-time random number on a **Waveform Chart** in LabVIEW. List the components used on the Functions Palette. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Auto-Indexing** and **Shift Registers** in a While Loop. How are they used to compute the **running average** of the last 5 samples of a temperature sensor? Provide the mathematical expression and a block-level diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Front Panel components** (Functions → Numeric + Modern → Graph):
1. A **Stop Button** (Boolean control) — to halt the loop.
2. A **Numeric Indicator** (Thermometer / Tank) — shows the latest scalar value.
3. A **Waveform Chart** — displays the time-history of the random numbers.

**Block Diagram components** (Programming → Structures + Numeric):
1. **While Loop** (Programming → Structures → While Loop).
2. **Random Number (0–1)** generator (Programming → Numeric → Random Number 0 to 1).
3. **Wait (ms)** function — to introduce a controllable time delay, e.g., **200 ms** (avoiding 100% CPU usage).
4. The **Stop Button** is wired to the **conditional terminal** (red stop sign).
5. The output of the **Random Number** node is wired to both the **Numeric Indicator** and the **Waveform Chart** (single-point input mode).

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
   [Random Number 0–1] ──┬──> [Numeric Indicator]
                         │
                         └──> [Waveform Chart (inside While Loop)]
   [Stop Button] ──────────> [Conditional Terminal]
   [Wait 200 ms] ──────────> [Iteration Terminal region]
```

**Block Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WL["WHILE LOOP BORDER"] --> I["i iteration terminal 0 to n"]
    R["Random Number 0 to 1"] --> N["Numeric Indicator outside loop"]
    R --> C["Waveform Chart inside loop auto indexed scalar"]
    S["Stop Button Boolean"] --> CT{"Conditional\nTerminal"}
    CT -- "FALSE keep looping" --> R
    style WL fill:#E0E0FF,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFD580,color:#000
    style CT fill:#FF6B6B,color:#000
```

**Working:** The loop executes the **Random Number** node each iteration. The generated scalar is sent to the **Waveform Chart** (which buffers and scrolls) and to the **Numeric Indicator** (showing the latest value). The loop continues until the user presses **Stop**, which makes the conditional terminal **TRUE** and exits the loop.

---

**Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Auto-Indexing** is a tunnel feature. When **enabled** on a Loop's output tunnel, it automatically **collects** scalars produced at each iteration into a **1-D array**. If **disabled**, only the *last* value exits the loop.

**Shift Registers** are loop-bound variables that:
- Pass the value from the **current** iteration to the **next** iteration.
- Are added by right-clicking the loop border → *Add Shift Register*.
- Have a terminal on the **right** (current value input) and a terminal on the **left** (value to be passed to next iteration).

**Mathematical Expression – Running Average of last 5 samples:**

Let $T_k$ be the temperature at iteration $k$. The running average over the previous 5 samples is:

$$\overline{T}_{k} = \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{j=0}^{4} T_{k-j}$$

This requires the loop to **store the last 5 values** → use **Shift Registers + Build Array** as a sliding window.

**Block Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SR["Shift Register Left side\nprevious values stored"] --> BA["Build Array function\nholds 5 element array"]
    BA --> SRR["Shift Register Right side\ninputs new value at next iteration"]
    T["Temperature Sensor reading T of k"] --> SRR
    SRR --> BA
    BA --> SUM["Compound Arithmetic\nSum of array elements"]
    SUM --> DIV["Divide by 5 constant"]
    DIV --> AVG["Running Average Indicator"]
    style SR fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style AVG fill:#FFD580,color:#000
```

**Working (step-by-step):**
1. The **right** shift register receives $T_k$.
2. **Build Array** appends $T_k$ to the array of previous 4 values.
3. The left shift register is updated with the new 5-element array.
4. **Compound Arithmetic (Sum)** sums the 5 elements.
5. **Divide by 5** computes the average $\overline{T}_{k}$.
6. The result is displayed on the **Running Average** indicator.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOSEN: Case + Sequence Structures**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCICL407]**

**(a)** Design a LabVIEW block diagram using a **Case Structure** that takes a numeric input (0 to 3) from the Front Panel and displays a corresponding waveform:
- Case 0 → DC value of **0 V**
- Case 1 → Sine wave of amplitude **5 V**, frequency **50 Hz**
- Case 2 → Square wave of amplitude **5 V**, frequency **50 Hz**
- Case 3 → Triangle wave of amplitude **5 V**, frequency **50 Hz**

The case selector is to be an **Enum** (or Integer) control. Provide the Front Panel layout, the Case Structure frames, and a sample XY Graph. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the use of a **Flat Sequence Structure** to perform the following operations **in order**: (1) Open/Create a data file, (2) Acquire 100 samples from a sensor, (3) Write samples to the file, (4) Close the file. Why is the order critical? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Front Panel:**
- A **Vertical Pointer Slide / Enum Control** labelled "Waveform Selector" with values `Sine`, `Square`, `Triangle`, `DC 0`.
- A **Graph indicator** (XY Graph or Waveform Chart).
- Numeric controls for **Amplitude (A)**, **Frequency (f)** in **Hz**, and **Samples (N)**.

**Block Diagram – Case Structure (4 cases + Default):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SEL["Enum Selector from Front Panel"] --> CS{"CASE STRUCTURE\nSelector Terminal"}
    CS -- "Sine" --> C1["Sine Waveform Generator VI\nAmplitude 5, Frequency 50"]
    CS -- "Square" --> C2["Square Waveform Generator VI\nAmplitude 5, Frequency 50"]
    CS -- "Triangle" --> C3["Triangle Waveform Generator VI\nAmplitude 5, Frequency 50"]
    CS -- "DC 0" --> C4["Constant 0.0 wired to output"]
    C1 --> GR["XY Graph or Waveform Chart"]
    C2 --> GR
    C3 --> GR
    C4 --> GR
    style SEL fill:#FFD580,color:#000
    style CS fill:#FFE0E0,color:#000
    style GR fill:#90EE90,color:#000
```

**Step-by-step Working:**
1. The user selects the waveform using the **Enum** on the Front Panel.
2. The enum's value is wired to the **selector terminal** (the `?` mark) on the Case Structure.
3. Only the case matching the enum label is **visible/executable** at a time; the others are greyed out.
4. The corresponding waveform generator VI produces a 1-D array of samples.
5. The array is wired out through a tunnel to the **Graph indicator**.

**Mathematical note:** Each waveform is a function of time $t = i \cdot T_{s}$ with $T_{s} = \dfrac{1}{f_{s}}$ and $N$ samples:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sine: } y_i &= 5 \cdot \sin(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t) \\
\text{Square: } y_i &= 5 \cdot \text{sgn}(\sin(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t)) \\
\text{Triangle: } y_i &= \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot 5 \cdot \arcsin(\sin(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t)) \\
\text{DC 0: } y_i &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Flat Sequence Structure – Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0["FRAME 0\nOpen or Create File\nFunction: Open or Create File"] --> F1["FRAME 1\nAcquire 100 Samples\nFor Loop with 100 iterations"]
    F1 --> F2["FRAME 2\nWrite to File\nFunction: Write to Text File"]
    F2 --> F3["FRAME 3\nClose File\nFunction: Close File"]
    style F0 fill:#E0FFE0,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#E0FFE0,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#E0FFE0,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#E0FFE0,color:#000
```

**Why the order is critical (4 logical points):**
1. **Frame 0 (Open File):** The file must be created/opened *first*; otherwise, Frames 1–3 have no valid file reference (refnum) to operate on.
2. **Frame 1 (Acquire Samples):** The sensor must be read *after* the file is ready so that the data can be buffered for writing.
3. **Frame 2 (Write Samples):** The data must be written *after* acquisition; writing before acquisition would write empty or stale data.
4. **Frame 3 (Close File):** The file must be closed *last*; closing it early would invalidate the refnum, and any subsequent write would fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Flat Sequence and not just a wire?** In LabVIEW's dataflow, if all four subVIs had no data dependency between them, they would execute **in parallel**. The Flat Sequence Structure *forces* them to run **strictly left-to-right**, which is essential here because the file refnum from Frame 0 is a *data dependency* — but using a Flat Sequence makes the temporal order explicit and readable for code maintenance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing For and While Loops** (–2 marks): A For Loop CANNOT be terminated mid-way using a Boolean; only the **N terminal** controls it.
> 2. **Iteration index starts at 0, not 1** (–1 mark): If the student writes $i = 1$ to $N$, the answer is technically wrong.
> 3. **Auto-Indexing rule** (–2 marks): Disabling auto-indexing on a For Loop returns only the **last** value, not an array. Students often forget to enable it.
> 4. **Waveform Chart vs. XY Graph** (–2 marks): Wiring a *single scalar* to an **XY Graph** causes a broken wire. XY Graphs require **two arrays**.
> 5. **Case Structure tunnel defaults** (–1 mark): If a tunnel has data flowing in, the *Default case* MUST provide a value for that tunnel, or the run arrow will be broken.
> 6. **Sequence Structure misuse** (–1 mark): Using a Stacked Sequence in a new design is penalised; prefer Flat Sequence or State Machine.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LabVIEW** = *Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench*; file extension is **`.vi`**.
- **Two loop structures:** **For Loop** (fixed `N`) and **While Loop** (condition-based stop).
- **Iteration terminal (i) in both loops starts at 0** and increments by 1.
- **Auto-Indexing** at a loop tunnel → builds a 1-D array; default is *enabled* for For Loop, *disabled* for While Loop.
- **Shift Registers** → store values between loop iterations (left = previous, right = current).
- **Waveform Chart** → real-time, single-point or array, *auto-X axis* (time), has *buffer* (default history = **1024** points).
- **XY Graph** → requires two arrays (X and Y), no buffer, used for **Lissajous** and **calibration curves**.
- **Case Structure** → if-then-else / switch-case equivalent; selector can be Boolean, Integer, String, or Enum; executes only one case.
- **Flat Sequence** → executes frames strictly **left → right**; used when order of operations is critical.
- **Stacked Sequence** → executes frames 0, 1, 2, … sequentially with local variables; **deprecated** for new designs.
- **Dataflow rule** → a node executes only when *all* its input wires carry valid data.
- **Wait (ms)** function is essential in While Loops to avoid 100% CPU usage — typical value: **100–500 ms**.
- **Sampling Theorem:** $f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Icons and Connectors** allow a VI to be used as a *Sub-VI* (function-call equivalent).
- **Auto-Indexing array length** equals the **loop count** $N$: $\vert Y \vert = N$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
